title: Week 7 Demo: Data Loading with pandas
section: Part 1: Reading CSV Files with pandas
text:
Part 1: Reading CSV Files with pandas
The manual parsing pipeline you’ve been writing (open(), split(), strip(), type conversion, try/except) is exactly what pd.read_csv() automates. This section introduces the function, shows what it handles automatically, and then walks through the parameters you’ll use most often to customize its behavior.

Creating a sample CSV file
First, we’ll create a CSV file using open(). This lets you see exactly what the raw file contains before pandas reads it.

# Create a CSV file with fleet registry data
with open('fleet_registry.csv', 'w') as f:
    f.write('ship_name,ship_class,crew_size,max_speed,launch_year\n')
    f.write('Aurora,Explorer,150,9.2,2371\n')
    f.write('Nebula Runner,Cargo,45,6.8,2368\n')
    f.write('Dark Comet,Fighter,12,9.8,2374\n')
    f.write('Star Whisper,Science,80,7.5,2370\n')
    f.write('Iron Horizon,Cargo,50,6.5,2365\n')
    f.write('Void Dancer,Explorer,120,9.0,2373\n')
    f.write('Solar Wind,Patrol,30,8.4,2369\n')
    f.write('Crimson Falcon,Fighter,15,9.6,2372\n')

# Verify: display the raw file contents
with open('fleet_registry.csv', 'r') as f:
    print(f.read())

ship_name,ship_class,crew_size,max_speed,launch_year
Aurora,Explorer,150,9.2,2371
Nebula Runner,Cargo,45,6.8,2368
Dark Comet,Fighter,12,9.8,2374
Star Whisper,Science,80,7.5,2370
Iron Horizon,Cargo,50,6.5,2365
Void Dancer,Explorer,120,9.0,2373
Solar Wind,Patrol,30,8.4,2369
Crimson Falcon,Fighter,15,9.6,2372



Understanding the file:
This is a standard CSV file. The first line is the header row with field names. Each subsequent line is one record with values separated by commas. In previous weeks, your next step would have been a parsing loop to split, strip, convert, and error-check each row.
With pandas, all of that is one line.


Your first pd.read_csv() call

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Read the CSV file into a DataFrame
fleet = pd.read_csv('fleet_registry.csv')
print(fleet)

        ship_name ship_class  crew_size  max_speed  launch_year
0          Aurora   Explorer        150        9.2         2371
1   Nebula Runner      Cargo         45        6.8         2368
2      Dark Comet    Fighter         12        9.8         2374
3    Star Whisper    Science         80        7.5         2370
4    Iron Horizon      Cargo         50        6.5         2365
5     Void Dancer   Explorer        120        9.0         2373
6      Solar Wind     Patrol         30        8.4         2369
7  Crimson Falcon    Fighter         15        9.6         2372


Understanding the output:
pd.read_csv() returns a DataFrame. Look at what it did automatically: - Detected the header row and used it for column names - Split each line on commas - Stripped whitespace - Inferred data types for each column
That’s the entire manual pipeline in a single function call.


Type inference
One important thing pd.read_csv() does automatically is figure out what type of data each column contains. Let’s check what it decided:

# Check what types pandas inferred
print(fleet.dtypes)

ship_name       object
ship_class      object
crew_size        int64
max_speed      float64
launch_year      int64
dtype: object


Understanding type inference:
pandas examined the values in each column and chose appropriate types: - ship_name and ship_class contain text → text type (displayed as object or str depending on your pandas version) - crew_size and launch_year contain whole numbers → int64 - max_speed contains decimal numbers → float64
Compare this to your manual approach, where you explicitly called int() or float() on each field. pd.read_csv() handles type conversion automatically. The textbook calls this type inference: pandas looks at the actual values and decides what type each column should be.
Type inference usually works well, but it’s not perfect. If a numeric column contains even one non-numeric value (like “N/A”), pandas will treat the entire column as text. We’ll see how to handle that later in this demo.


Customizing read_csv with parameters
The basic pd.read_csv('filename.csv') call works when the file is well-formatted with a header row and comma delimiters. But real-world files are not always that clean. pd.read_csv() has many parameters to handle different file formats. The textbook notes that it has around 50 parameters, and you don’t need to memorize them. Here are the ones you’ll use most often.

Reading a file without a header row
Not every CSV file has a header row. What happens if we read one without telling pandas?

# Create a CSV file with no header row
with open('fleet_no_header.csv', 'w') as f:
    f.write('Aurora,Explorer,150,9.2,2371\n')
    f.write('Nebula Runner,Cargo,45,6.8,2368\n')
    f.write('Dark Comet,Fighter,12,9.8,2374\n')
    f.write('Star Whisper,Science,80,7.5,2370\n')

# Read without specifying that there's no header
fleet_bad = pd.read_csv('fleet_no_header.csv')
print("Without header=None (wrong):")
print(fleet_bad)

Without header=None (wrong):
          Aurora Explorer  150  9.2  2371
0  Nebula Runner    Cargo   45  6.8  2368
1     Dark Comet  Fighter   12  9.8  2374
2   Star Whisper  Science   80  7.5  2370


Understanding the problem:
By default, pd.read_csv() assumes the first row is the header. Since this file has no header, pandas used the first data row (“Aurora,Explorer,150,9.2,2371”) as column names. That first record is now lost from the data, and the column names are meaningless.

# Fix: tell pandas there's no header row
fleet_no_header = pd.read_csv('fleet_no_header.csv', header=None)
print("With header=None:")
print(fleet_no_header)

With header=None:
               0         1    2    3     4
0         Aurora  Explorer  150  9.2  2371
1  Nebula Runner     Cargo   45  6.8  2368
2     Dark Comet   Fighter   12  9.8  2374
3   Star Whisper   Science   80  7.5  2370


Understanding header=None:
Setting header=None tells pandas that no row in the file contains column names. pandas assigns default integer column names (0, 1, 2, 3, 4) instead. The data is correct now, and all four records are present.

# Better: provide your own column names
fleet_named = pd.read_csv('fleet_no_header.csv', header=None,
                           names=['ship_name', 'ship_class', 'crew_size', 
                                  'max_speed', 'launch_year'])
print("With custom column names:")
print(fleet_named)

With custom column names:
       ship_name ship_class  crew_size  max_speed  launch_year
0         Aurora   Explorer        150        9.2         2371
1  Nebula Runner      Cargo         45        6.8         2368
2     Dark Comet    Fighter         12        9.8         2374
3   Star Whisper    Science         80        7.5         2370


Understanding the names parameter:
The names parameter lets you supply column names when the file doesn’t have them. You pass a list of strings with one name per column, in order. This produces the same clean result as reading a file that already has a header.


Setting the index during loading
In Week 5, you learned to use .set_index() to make a column the row index after loading. You can also do this during loading with the index_col parameter.

# Use a column as the row index during loading
fleet_indexed = pd.read_csv('fleet_registry.csv', index_col='ship_name')
print(fleet_indexed)

               ship_class  crew_size  max_speed  launch_year
ship_name                                                   
Aurora           Explorer        150        9.2         2371
Nebula Runner       Cargo         45        6.8         2368
Dark Comet        Fighter         12        9.8         2374
Star Whisper      Science         80        7.5         2370
Iron Horizon        Cargo         50        6.5         2365
Void Dancer      Explorer        120        9.0         2373
Solar Wind         Patrol         30        8.4         2369
Crimson Falcon    Fighter         15        9.6         2372


Understanding index_col:
The index_col parameter tells pd.read_csv() which column should become the row labels. The ship_name column is no longer a regular column; it’s now the index. You can use .loc['Aurora'] to select Aurora’s row by label, just like you did with student names in the Week 5 demo.


Selecting specific columns
Sometimes a file has more columns than you need. Rather than loading everything and then dropping columns, you can tell pandas to load only the columns you want.

# Load only certain columns
fleet_subset = pd.read_csv('fleet_registry.csv', 
                            usecols=['ship_name', 'ship_class', 'crew_size'])
print(fleet_subset)

        ship_name ship_class  crew_size
0          Aurora   Explorer        150
1   Nebula Runner      Cargo         45
2      Dark Comet    Fighter         12
3    Star Whisper    Science         80
4    Iron Horizon      Cargo         50
5     Void Dancer   Explorer        120
6      Solar Wind     Patrol         30
7  Crimson Falcon    Fighter         15


Understanding usecols:
usecols loads just the columns you specify. This saves memory and keeps your DataFrame focused on the data you actually need. You pass a list of column names (matching the header row) to select.


Reading a limited number of rows
When working with a large file, you may want to peek at the first few rows before loading the entire dataset. The nrows parameter handles this.

# Read only the first 3 rows
fleet_preview = pd.read_csv('fleet_registry.csv', nrows=3)
print(fleet_preview)

       ship_name ship_class  crew_size  max_speed  launch_year
0         Aurora   Explorer        150        9.2         2371
1  Nebula Runner      Cargo         45        6.8         2368
2     Dark Comet    Fighter         12        9.8         2374


Understanding nrows:
The nrows parameter tells pd.read_csv() to read only that many data rows (the header is always read). This is useful for checking that your parameters are correct before committing to a full load.



Handling missing data during loading
Real-world data often has missing values. They might appear as empty fields, placeholder text like “UNKNOWN”, or sentinel values like “-999”. pandas can detect and handle these during loading.

# Create a CSV file with missing and problematic values
with open('fleet_missing.csv', 'w') as f:
    f.write('ship_name,ship_class,crew_size,max_speed,launch_year\n')
    f.write('Aurora,Explorer,150,9.2,2371\n')
    f.write('Nebula Runner,Cargo,,6.8,2368\n')
    f.write('Dark Comet,Fighter,12,UNKNOWN,2374\n')
    f.write('Star Whisper,Science,80,7.5,\n')
    f.write('Iron Horizon,Cargo,50,6.5,2365\n')
    f.write('Void Dancer,Explorer,MISSING,9.0,2373\n')

# Read with default settings
fleet_messy = pd.read_csv('fleet_missing.csv')
print("Default reading:")
print(fleet_messy)
print("\nData types:")
print(fleet_messy.dtypes)

Default reading:
       ship_name ship_class crew_size max_speed  launch_year
0         Aurora   Explorer       150       9.2       2371.0
1  Nebula Runner      Cargo       NaN       6.8       2368.0
2     Dark Comet    Fighter        12   UNKNOWN       2374.0
3   Star Whisper    Science        80       7.5          NaN
4   Iron Horizon      Cargo        50       6.5       2365.0
5    Void Dancer   Explorer   MISSING       9.0       2373.0

Data types:
ship_name       object
ship_class      object
crew_size       object
max_speed       object
launch_year    float64
dtype: object


Understanding the problem:
Look at the data types. crew_size and max_speed should be numeric, but some rows contain text placeholders (“UNKNOWN”, “MISSING”) or empty fields. pandas handles empty fields automatically by inserting NaN and adjusting the column type. But it does not recognize custom placeholders like “UNKNOWN” or “MISSING” as missing values. When even one value in a column is text, pandas treats the entire column as a text type, which means you cannot compute statistics on it.
In your manual parsing pipeline, this is where try/except would catch the ValueError when you tried to convert “UNKNOWN” to a number. pd.read_csv() has its own way to handle this.

# Tell pandas what values represent missing data
fleet_clean = pd.read_csv('fleet_missing.csv', 
                           na_values=['UNKNOWN', 'MISSING'])
print("With na_values:")
print(fleet_clean)
print("\nData types:")
print(fleet_clean.dtypes)

With na_values:
       ship_name ship_class  crew_size  max_speed  launch_year
0         Aurora   Explorer      150.0        9.2       2371.0
1  Nebula Runner      Cargo        NaN        6.8       2368.0
2     Dark Comet    Fighter       12.0        NaN       2374.0
3   Star Whisper    Science       80.0        7.5          NaN
4   Iron Horizon      Cargo       50.0        6.5       2365.0
5    Void Dancer   Explorer        NaN        9.0       2373.0

Data types:
ship_name       object
ship_class      object
crew_size      float64
max_speed      float64
launch_year    float64
dtype: object


Understanding na_values:
The na_values parameter tells pandas which additional strings should be treated as missing data. pandas already recognizes some common missing-value markers by default (like empty fields and a few standard placeholders). But domain-specific placeholders like “UNKNOWN” or “MISSING” need to be specified explicitly.
When pandas encounters these values during loading, it replaces them with NaN (Not a Number). This is the same NaN you saw during automatic alignment in Week 5.
Now look at the data types. With the text placeholders converted to NaN, pandas can correctly infer numeric types for crew_size and max_speed (both float64). They are float64 rather than int64 because NaN is a floating-point value. Any integer column with missing values automatically becomes float to accommodate NaN.
na_values is doing something similar to your try/except blocks. It identifies values that cannot be used as data and marks them as missing rather than crashing the process. The difference is that pd.read_csv() handles this at the column level during loading, while your manual approach handled it row by row.


Writing DataFrames to CSV
So far, we’ve focused on reading data into pandas. But the workflow often goes both directions: you load data, process it, and then save the results. Just as pd.read_csv() reads data in, to_csv() writes data out.

# Read the clean fleet data
fleet = pd.read_csv('fleet_registry.csv')

# Write to a new CSV file
fleet.to_csv('fleet_output.csv')

# Verify: check what was written
with open('fleet_output.csv', 'r') as f:
    print(f.read())

,ship_name,ship_class,crew_size,max_speed,launch_year
0,Aurora,Explorer,150,9.2,2371
1,Nebula Runner,Cargo,45,6.8,2368
2,Dark Comet,Fighter,12,9.8,2374
3,Star Whisper,Science,80,7.5,2370
4,Iron Horizon,Cargo,50,6.5,2365
5,Void Dancer,Explorer,120,9.0,2373
6,Solar Wind,Patrol,30,8.4,2369
7,Crimson Falcon,Fighter,15,9.6,2372



Understanding the output:
Notice the extra column at the beginning. That’s the row index (0, 1, 2, …). By default, to_csv() writes the index as the first column. This is often not what you want.

# Write without the index
fleet.to_csv('fleet_output_clean.csv', index=False)

# Verify: check the cleaner output
with open('fleet_output_clean.csv', 'r') as f:
    print(f.read())

ship_name,ship_class,crew_size,max_speed,launch_year
Aurora,Explorer,150,9.2,2371
Nebula Runner,Cargo,45,6.8,2368
Dark Comet,Fighter,12,9.8,2374
Star Whisper,Science,80,7.5,2370
Iron Horizon,Cargo,50,6.5,2365
Void Dancer,Explorer,120,9.0,2373
Solar Wind,Patrol,30,8.4,2369
Crimson Falcon,Fighter,15,9.6,2372



Understanding index=False:
Setting index=False tells pandas to skip writing the row index. The output is now a clean CSV file that matches the format of the original input. This round-trip (read, process, write) is a common workflow: load data, analyze or transform it, and save the results.

title: Week 7 Demo: Data Loading with pandas
text:
Part 2: Working with JSON Data
Part 1 covered CSV files. But CSV is not the only data format you’ll encounter. JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the standard format for data on the web. When you request data from a web API (which we’ll do in Part 3), the response almost always comes back as JSON. Understanding JSON now prepares you for that workflow.

What JSON looks like
JSON is built from structures you already know from Python: - JSON objects are like Python dictionaries: {"key": "value"} - JSON arrays are like Python lists: [1, 2, 3] - Objects and arrays can be nested inside each other

# A JSON string representing planetary survey data
json_string = '''[
    {"planet": "Verdantis", "sector": "Alpha-7", "atmosphere": "nitrogen-oxygen", "avg_temp_c": 22, "moons": 2},
    {"planet": "Glacius Prime", "sector": "Beta-3", "atmosphere": "thin nitrogen", "avg_temp_c": -45, "moons": 0},
    {"planet": "Pyraxis", "sector": "Alpha-7", "atmosphere": "carbon dioxide", "avg_temp_c": 310, "moons": 1},
    {"planet": "Aquillon", "sector": "Gamma-1", "atmosphere": "nitrogen-oxygen", "avg_temp_c": 15, "moons": 3},
    {"planet": "Dusthaven", "sector": "Beta-3", "atmosphere": "thin carbon dioxide", "avg_temp_c": -10, "moons": 0}
]'''

print(json_string)

[
    {"planet": "Verdantis", "sector": "Alpha-7", "atmosphere": "nitrogen-oxygen", "avg_temp_c": 22, "moons": 2},
    {"planet": "Glacius Prime", "sector": "Beta-3", "atmosphere": "thin nitrogen", "avg_temp_c": -45, "moons": 0},
    {"planet": "Pyraxis", "sector": "Alpha-7", "atmosphere": "carbon dioxide", "avg_temp_c": 310, "moons": 1},
    {"planet": "Aquillon", "sector": "Gamma-1", "atmosphere": "nitrogen-oxygen", "avg_temp_c": 15, "moons": 3},
    {"planet": "Dusthaven", "sector": "Beta-3", "atmosphere": "thin carbon dioxide", "avg_temp_c": -10, "moons": 0}
]


Understanding the structure:
This JSON string contains an array (list) of objects (dictionaries). Each object represents one planet with the same set of keys. If this looks familiar, it should: this is exactly the “list of dictionaries” pattern from Week 2, written as text.
The difference between JSON and CSV is flexibility. CSV requires a flat table with consistent columns, while JSON can represent nested structures. For example, a planet could have a list of moons, each with its own properties. That flexibility is why JSON is the standard for web data.


Python’s json module
Python has a built-in json module for converting between JSON strings and Python objects.

import json

# Parse JSON string into Python objects
planets = json.loads(json_string)

# It's now a regular Python list of dictionaries
print(f"Type: {type(planets)}")
print(f"Number of records: {len(planets)}")
print(f"\nFirst planet:")
print(planets[0])

Type: <class 'list'>
Number of records: 5

First planet:
{'planet': 'Verdantis', 'sector': 'Alpha-7', 'atmosphere': 'nitrogen-oxygen', 'avg_temp_c': 22, 'moons': 2}


Understanding json.loads():
json.loads() (the “s” stands for “string”) takes a JSON string and converts it to Python objects: - JSON objects become Python dictionaries - JSON arrays become Python lists - JSON strings become Python strings - JSON numbers become Python ints or floats
After parsing, planets is a regular Python list of dictionaries. You can loop through it, access dictionary keys, and work with it using all the Python skills you already have.

# Access individual records and fields
print("Planet names:")
for planet in planets:
    print(f"  {planet['planet']} - {planet['atmosphere']}")

# Convert back to a formatted JSON string
formatted = json.dumps(planets, indent=2)
print(f"\nFormatted JSON (first 200 characters):")
print(formatted[:200])

Planet names:
  Verdantis - nitrogen-oxygen
  Glacius Prime - thin nitrogen
  Pyraxis - carbon dioxide
  Aquillon - nitrogen-oxygen
  Dusthaven - thin carbon dioxide

Formatted JSON (first 200 characters):
[
  {
    "planet": "Verdantis",
    "sector": "Alpha-7",
    "atmosphere": "nitrogen-oxygen",
    "avg_temp_c": 22,
    "moons": 2
  },
  {
    "planet": "Glacius Prime",
    "sector": "Beta-3",
    


Understanding json.dumps():
json.dumps() converts Python objects back to a JSON string. The indent parameter adds formatting with line breaks and indentation, making the output human-readable. Without indent, the entire JSON would be on one line.


pandas JSON support
Since a list of dictionaries with consistent keys is essentially tabular data, pandas can convert it directly to a DataFrame.

# Convert the parsed JSON (list of dicts) to a DataFrame
planets_df = pd.DataFrame(planets)
print(planets_df)

          planet   sector           atmosphere  avg_temp_c  moons
0      Verdantis  Alpha-7      nitrogen-oxygen          22      2
1  Glacius Prime   Beta-3        thin nitrogen         -45      0
2        Pyraxis  Alpha-7       carbon dioxide         310      1
3       Aquillon  Gamma-1      nitrogen-oxygen          15      3
4      Dusthaven   Beta-3  thin carbon dioxide         -10      0


Understanding the conversion:
This is the same pd.DataFrame() call from Week 5. You pass a list of dictionaries where each dictionary becomes a row and the keys become column names. The JSON parsing step (json.loads()) gave you a list of dictionaries, and pandas knows how to handle that directly.
pandas also has pd.read_json() which can read JSON files directly into a DataFrame. Let’s write our data to a JSON file first, then read it:

# Write the JSON string to a file
with open('planets.json', 'w') as f:
    f.write(json_string)

# Read the JSON file directly into a DataFrame
planets_direct = pd.read_json('planets.json')
print(planets_direct)

          planet   sector           atmosphere  avg_temp_c  moons
0      Verdantis  Alpha-7      nitrogen-oxygen          22      2
1  Glacius Prime   Beta-3        thin nitrogen         -45      0
2        Pyraxis  Alpha-7       carbon dioxide         310      1
3       Aquillon  Gamma-1      nitrogen-oxygen          15      3
4      Dusthaven   Beta-3  thin carbon dioxide         -10      0


Understanding pd.read_json():
pd.read_json() combines the parsing and DataFrame creation into one step, similar to how pd.read_csv() handles CSV files. For simple, tabular JSON (a list of objects with consistent keys), this works well. For more complex or nested JSON structures, you may need to use json.loads() first to extract the parts you need, then pass those to pd.DataFrame().


Writing JSON

# Write a DataFrame to a JSON file
planets_df.to_json('planets_output.json', orient='records', indent=2)

# Verify: check what was written
with open('planets_output.json', 'r') as f:
    print(f.read())

[
  {
    "planet":"Verdantis",
    "sector":"Alpha-7",
    "atmosphere":"nitrogen-oxygen",
    "avg_temp_c":22,
    "moons":2
  },
  {
    "planet":"Glacius Prime",
    "sector":"Beta-3",
    "atmosphere":"thin nitrogen",
    "avg_temp_c":-45,
    "moons":0
  },
  {
    "planet":"Pyraxis",
    "sector":"Alpha-7",
    "atmosphere":"carbon dioxide",
    "avg_temp_c":310,
    "moons":1
  },
  {
    "planet":"Aquillon",
    "sector":"Gamma-1",
    "atmosphere":"nitrogen-oxygen",
    "avg_temp_c":15,
    "moons":3
  },
  {
    "planet":"Dusthaven",
    "sector":"Beta-3",
    "atmosphere":"thin carbon dioxide",
    "avg_temp_c":-10,
    "moons":0
  }
]


Understanding to_json():
to_json() writes a DataFrame to JSON format. The orient parameter controls the structure of the output: - orient='records' produces a list of dictionaries, one per row. This is the most common format and matches the structure we started with. - indent=2 adds formatting for readability.
Other orient options exist (like 'columns' or 'index'), but 'records' is the most widely used because it matches the format that web APIs typically produce and consume.


When to use json module vs pd.read_json()
Use pd.read_json() when: - The JSON is a simple list of objects (flat, tabular structure) - You want a DataFrame immediately
Use json.loads() + pd.DataFrame() when: - The JSON has nested structures you need to navigate - You only want part of the JSON data - You need to inspect the data before converting
In practice, web API responses often have extra metadata wrapped around the actual data. You’ll use json.loads() (or the .json() method on a response object) to get to the data you want, then convert that portion to a DataFrame.

title: Week 7 Demo: Data Loading with pandas
text:
Part 3: Interacting with Web APIs
Parts 1 and 2 covered loading data from local files, both CSV and JSON. But a large amount of data is available through web APIs (Application Programming Interfaces). A web API lets you request specific data from a server over the internet and receive a structured response, usually in JSON format. This is where the JSON skills from Part 2 become directly practical.

What a web API is
When you visit a website, your browser sends a request to a server and gets back an HTML page for humans to read. A web API works the same way, but the response is structured data (usually JSON) designed for programs to process.
The pattern is straightforward: 1. Send an HTTP request to a URL (the API endpoint) 2. Receive a response containing structured data 3. Parse the response (usually JSON) 4. Work with the data in Python


Making an API request
Python’s requests library makes HTTP requests simple. We’ll use the GitHub API as an example. It provides public data about GitHub repositories without requiring an account or API key.
The following code cells require internet access to reach the GitHub API. If you are working offline or the request fails, read through the code and explanations to understand the pattern. The concepts (make a request, check the response, parse JSON, convert to DataFrame) are what matter. You can re-run these cells later when you have internet access.

import requests

# Request recent issues from the pandas GitHub repository
# (This is the same API endpoint used in the textbook's Chapter 6 example)
url = 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues'
resp = requests.get(url)
resp.raise_for_status()

print(f"Status code: {resp.status_code}")
print(f"Response type: {type(resp.json())}")
print(f"Number of items: {len(resp.json())}")

Status code: 200
Response type: <class 'list'>
Number of items: 30


If import requests gives a ModuleNotFoundError, you need to install the library. Run pip install requests in your terminal, then restart your kernel and try again.
Understanding the request:
requests.get(url) sends an HTTP GET request to the GitHub API. This is the same kind of request your browser makes when you visit a web page, but instead of HTML, the server returns JSON data.
resp.raise_for_status() checks whether the request succeeded. If something went wrong (server error, invalid URL, rate limit exceeded), this raises an exception. This is similar to how you use try/except to catch errors in file processing. A status code of 200 means success.
resp.json() parses the JSON response into Python objects (a list of dictionaries in this case). This is equivalent to calling json.loads() on the response text, which you used in Part 2.


From API response to DataFrame
Now that we have a successful response, let’s see what the API returned and convert it to a DataFrame. The GitHub API returns a list of dictionaries, one per issue. Each dictionary contains many fields.

# Parse the JSON response
data = resp.json()

# Look at the keys available in one issue
print("Available fields in each issue:")
print(sorted(data[0].keys()))

Available fields in each issue:
['active_lock_reason', 'assignee', 'assignees', 'author_association', 'body', 'closed_at', 'closed_by', 'comments', 'comments_url', 'created_at', 'draft', 'events_url', 'html_url', 'id', 'labels', 'labels_url', 'locked', 'milestone', 'node_id', 'number', 'performed_via_github_app', 'pull_request', 'reactions', 'repository_url', 'state', 'state_reason', 'timeline_url', 'title', 'type', 'updated_at', 'url', 'user']


Understanding the response:
Each issue is a dictionary with many fields: title, body, user information, labels, timestamps, and more. Most API responses contain more data than you need. The next step is to convert to a DataFrame and select the relevant columns.

# Convert to DataFrame
issues = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Select useful columns
issues_subset = issues[['number', 'title', 'state', 'created_at']]
print(issues_subset.head(10))

   number                                              title state  \
0   65083   Refactor source file inspection logic in conf.py  open   
1   65082  DOC: Fix incorrect source link for DataFrame d...  open   
2   65078  DOC: clarify bool vs int behavior in factorize...  open   
3   65076  DOC: Fix dark mode text color in tutorial data...  open   
4   65075                 CI: Build documentation using pixi  open   
5   65072                                 Improve dropna doc  open   
6   65071  DOC: Add comprehensive merge join examples for...  open   
7   65069              BUG: fixed Interval to_json/read_json  open   
8   65067  WARN: add FutureWarning when DataFrame.update(...  open   
9   65066  BUG: escape SQL identifiers in ADBCDatabase to...  open   

             created_at  
0  2026-04-05T15:02:15Z  
1  2026-04-05T13:25:05Z  
2  2026-04-05T05:36:21Z  
3  2026-04-04T22:28:35Z  
4  2026-04-04T21:19:58Z  
5  2026-04-04T17:18:38Z  
6  2026-04-04T17:03:53Z  
7  2026-04-04T14:21:28Z  
8  2026-04-04T07:44:44Z  
9  2026-04-04T07:28:35Z  


Understanding the workflow:
This is the same pd.DataFrame() call you’ve been using. A list of dictionaries becomes a DataFrame where each dictionary is a row. Then we use column selection (double brackets from Week 5) to keep just the columns we need.
The result is a clean, labeled DataFrame created from live data on the internet. You went from a URL to an analyzable DataFrame in a few lines of code.
The specific issues shown depend on when you run this code. GitHub issues are created and updated constantly, so your output will differ from someone who runs this at a different time.


A note about APIs
The GitHub API we used here is public and doesn’t require authentication for basic requests. But there are important practical considerations.
Most APIs limit how many requests you can make per hour. The GitHub API allows 60 requests per hour without authentication. If you exceed the limit, requests will fail temporarily.
Many APIs also require an API key or account credentials. This is how services control access and track usage. The textbook covers this briefly, and you’ll encounter it when working with data from services like Twitter, weather providers, or financial data platforms.
Not all APIs return simple lists of dictionaries, either. Some wrap data in metadata, use pagination for large results, or nest data in complex structures. The json.loads() + pd.DataFrame() approach gives you more control for these cases than pd.read_json() alone.

title: Week 7 Demo: Data Loading with pandas
text:
Conclusion

What You’ve Learned
This demo introduced the core data loading tools from Chapter 6.
For CSV files, you learned to use pd.read_csv() to read files into DataFrames with automatic header detection and type inference. You saw how to customize loading with parameters (header, names, index_col, usecols, nrows, na_values), how to handle missing data and sentinel values during loading, and how to write DataFrames back to CSV with to_csv().
For JSON data, you learned how JSON structures map to Python dictionaries and lists, how to use Python’s json module (json.loads() to parse, json.dumps() to write), and how pd.read_json() can convert JSON files directly into DataFrames.
For web APIs, you learned how to use requests.get() to fetch data from a URL, parse the JSON response, and convert it to a DataFrame. You also saw practical considerations like rate limits and authentication.


The Manual Parsing Connection
Every step in the manual parsing pipeline you’ve been writing (opening files, splitting fields, stripping whitespace, converting types, detecting headers, handling bad values, building the result) has a corresponding behavior inside pd.read_csv(). Understanding what happens under the hood makes you a better analyst. When pd.read_csv() doesn’t behave as expected, whether it’s wrong types, missing values not detected, or columns misaligned, you’ll know where to look because you’ve done each of those steps manually.


What the Textbook Covers Next
Chapter 6 continues with data formats and tools not covered in this demo. These are worth knowing about, even if you don’t use them immediately.
pd.read_html() extracts tables from web pages into DataFrames. It’s useful for scraping tabular data from websites, though it requires additional libraries (lxml, beautifulsoup4).
pd.read_excel() reads .xlsx and .xls files and works similarly to pd.read_csv() with comparable parameters. to_excel() handles writing.
The chapter also covers binary storage formats: Pickle (Python’s built-in serialization, fast but Python-only), HDF5 (hierarchical data format for large scientific datasets), and Parquet (columnar format optimized for analytics, increasingly common in industry). These formats are more efficient than CSV for large datasets.
Finally, pd.read_sql() lets you run SQL queries and return the results as DataFrames, connecting pandas to relational databases like SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL through the SQLAlchemy library.
Each of these follows the same pattern: pd.read_*() to load data into a DataFrame, and a corresponding to_*() method to write data back out. Once you understand the read_csv() workflow, the other loading functions feel familiar.

title: Week 8 Assignment
text:
Submission location: All items are submitted in D2L (Week 8 Dropbox).
Reading: Chapters 1–6 (cumulative).
Datasets:

title: Week 8 Assignment
text:
The Situation
You work part-time at Hexagon Games, a small board game store that also hosts weekly game nights. The owner, Dana, is preparing for a quarterly business review with the store’s co-investor. She needs a clear picture of recent sales performance and wants the store’s game catalog organized into a usable format for inventory decisions.
The store’s point-of-sale system exports transaction data as a CSV file. Each record should contain seven fields: date, game name, quantity sold, unit price, customer type (member or walk-in), payment method, and the staff member who handled the sale. Dana has mentioned that the POS system sometimes logs incomplete transactions — missing payment methods, placeholder values where the staff member forgot to scan a membership card, and a few records that just didn’t export correctly.
The store also has a game catalog file from their distributor. This file is in JSON format and contains detailed information about each game in stock: name, category, player count range, estimated play time, complexity rating, and a list of game mechanics. The catalog has more structure than a simple spreadsheet — some fields contain nested information rather than single values.
Dana needs two things from you. First, a sales analysis from the CSV data that she can present at the review. Second, the catalog data pulled into a clean table so she can use it to evaluate which categories and complexity levels are represented in the store’s inventory.

title: Week 8 Assignment
text:
What You Need to Deliver
Your analysis must address the following requirements. Use the skills and methods from Weeks 1–7 to complete this work.

Part 1: Sales Data (CSV)

Data Loading and Quality Assessment
Load the sales log using pd.read_csv(). The file contains some placeholder values for missing data. After loading, inspect the result to assess data quality. If columns have unexpected data types or values that should be missing aren’t being treated as missing, determine which pd.read_csv() parameter can address this and reload the file. Your report should include:

The shape of the loaded DataFrame (rows and columns)
The output of .info() showing column types and non-null counts
Which columns contain missing values and how many are missing in each
Which columns changed data type because of missing values (and what type they became)
A look at the first few rows to verify the data loaded correctly



Sales Analysis
Dana needs to understand the store’s recent performance. Using the loaded DataFrame:

Compute summary statistics for unit price and quantity using .describe()
Create a new column calculating revenue for each transaction (quantity multiplied by unit price)
Show the ten highest-revenue transactions, sorted from highest to lowest
Count how many transactions came from each customer type
Calculate the average revenue for member transactions versus walk-in transactions



Multi-Condition Investigation
Dana has noticed that large walk-in purchases might represent event organizers buying for game nights at other venues. She wants to investigate transactions where the customer type was walk-in AND the quantity sold was 3 or more. For this subset:

How many transactions meet both conditions?
What is the average revenue for these transactions?
What games appear in this group?



Save Cleaned Results
Save the DataFrame with the new revenue column to a CSV file called sales_cleaned.csv. Make sure the default row index is not written to the file.



Part 2: Game Catalog (JSON)

Load and Inspect the Catalog
Load the game catalog JSON file using Python’s json module. The catalog has a nested structure — not every field is a simple value. After loading:

Show the raw Python structure for one game entry so you can see how the data is organized
Identify which fields are simple values and which are nested (lists or dictionaries)
Explain briefly (in a markdown cell) what the nested fields contain



Build a Catalog DataFrame
Extract the catalog data into a pandas DataFrame. Because of the nested structure, pd.read_json() will not handle this cleanly — you will need to work through the data manually. The catalog contains fields that are nested — lists or dictionaries inside each game entry. Your DataFrame must include information from these nested fields, not just the simple ones. For each nested field, decide how to represent it: extract a specific value (like minimum player count from a player count range) or convert a list into a comma-separated string. After creating the DataFrame:

Show the shape and column names
Display the first few rows
Show .dtypes to confirm the columns have appropriate types



Catalog Analysis
With the catalog in a DataFrame, answer the following for Dana’s inventory review:

How many games are in each category?
What are the summary statistics for complexity rating using .describe()?

title: Week 11 Assignment
text:
The Situation
You have been asked to help the Maplewood Parks and Recreation Department prepare for their spring session review. The department runs eight programs across four categories: Aquatics, Fitness, Arts, and Youth Sports. Each season, the front desk coordinator, Renee, exports registration data from the department’s shared spreadsheet so the program director can review enrollment numbers, instructor workloads, and outstanding payments before the session begins.
Renee flagged a few things when she sent the file. The spreadsheet has been updated by several staff members throughout the enrollment period, and not everyone followed the same conventions. Some fields were left blank when information was not yet available; others were filled with something temporary and never updated. Fee amounts for a handful of participants were still marked as pending at the time of entry. There was also a known system issue earlier in the season that caused two registrations to be submitted twice before it was caught and fixed.
The program director needs answers, but the data is not ready to be analyzed as-is. Your job is to load the file, assess what you find, resolve what needs to be resolved, and then answer the questions the department needs for their review.

Part 2: Clean the Data

A cleaning decision without a justification is not complete.
Your cleaning should address each of the following:

Column types that do not match the data they contain and why pandas inferred the type it did

Values that are present but not usable for analysis as-is

Duplicate records

When you are done, verify the DataFrame is in the expected state before moving on.


Part 3: Analysis
With a clean DataFrame, answer the following questions for the department’s spring review. All analysis must use methods covered in Chapters 1–7. If a method feels unfamiliar or was not used in prior weeks, it is likely out of scope. Use filtering, aggregation, and DataFrame operations you have already practiced.
Enrollment summary. How many registrations does each program have? Show counts sorted from highest to lowest.
Age group breakdown. Group participants into three age categories: Youth (under 18), Adult (18 to 60), and Senior (60 and older). Show how many registrations fall into each group, and then show how those groups break down across each program category.
Fees by category. How much in registration fees has been collected for each of the four program categories? Show one total per category.
Instructor load. How many registrations is each instructor responsible for, including records where no instructor was assigned?
Outstanding payments. How many registrations have not been fully paid, and what is the total amount still outstanding?


Save Your Results
Save the cleaned DataFrame to a file called registrations_cleaned.csv. Do not write the row index to the file.

title: Week 12 Demo: Combining and Reshaping DataFrames
text:
Introduction
Every DataFrame operation you have used in this course (loading, cleaning, filtering, aggregating) has worked with a single table. That is often not how real data arrives. A database might store entity records in one table and event records in another. A data export might produce separate files for different time periods or geographic regions. A reporting system might deliver measurements in a wide format where each column is a category rather than each row being a single observation.
Chapter 8 introduces the tools pandas provides to work with data spread across multiple sources: pd.concat() for stacking DataFrames that share the same structure, pd.merge() for joining DataFrames on shared keys, and pd.melt() for reshaping wide data into long format.
If you have taken database modeling, pd.merge() will look familiar. It implements the same join logic as SQL: matching rows from two tables based on a shared key column. The how parameter controls the join type (inner, left, right, or outer) and maps directly to the SQL JOIN types you already know.

title: Week 12 Demo: Combining and Reshaping DataFrames
text:
Setup
Before running any cells, download the following files from the course page and place them in your week12 folder alongside this notebook:

entity_registry.csv
incident_reports_north.csv
incident_reports_south.csv
monthly_sightings.csv


import pandas as pd

title: Week 12 Demo: Combining and Reshaping DataFrames
text:
Part 1: Stacking DataFrames with pd.concat()

The scenario
The Bureau of Paranormal Investigation maintains two regional offices. The Northern Bureau and the Southern Bureau each export their incident reports as separate CSV files. Before any analysis can run, these two files need to be combined into a single working table.
This is the simplest case for combining DataFrames: two tables with identical column structure that need to be stacked on top of each other.


Load the regional files

north = pd.read_csv("incident_reports_north.csv")
south = pd.read_csv("incident_reports_south.csv")

print("Northern Bureau:")
print(north.shape)
print(north.head(3))

Northern Bureau:
(15, 7)
  incident_id entity_id   location        date   investigator  \
0     INC-001   ENT-001     Arkham  2024-03-12   Dr. Armitage   
1     INC-002   ENT-002    Dunwich  2024-03-15   Dr. Armitage   
2     INC-003   ENT-001  Innsmouth  2024-04-02  Agent Peaslee   

   anomaly_severity containment_status  
0                 5             Active  
1                 4            Unknown  
2                 5             Active  



print("Southern Bureau:")
print(south.shape)
print(south.head(3))

Southern Bureau:
(12, 7)
  incident_id entity_id           location        date investigator  \
0     INC-016   ENT-007  Miskatonic Valley  2024-03-08  Agent Waite   
1     INC-017   ENT-001         Providence  2024-03-20  Agent Waite   
2     INC-018   ENT-002              Salem  2024-04-05   Dr. Morgan   

   anomaly_severity containment_status  
0                 3          Contained  
1                 5             Active  
2                 4            Unknown  


Understanding the output:
Both DataFrames have the same seven columns: incident_id, entity_id, location, date, investigator, anomaly_severity, and containment_status. The Northern Bureau has 15 incidents; the Southern Bureau has 12.
Notice that each DataFrame has its own default index starting at 0. When you stack them, pandas needs to decide what to do with those indexes.


Concatenate with pd.concat()

all_incidents = pd.concat([north, south], ignore_index=True)

print(f"Combined: {all_incidents.shape}")
print(all_incidents.head(3))
print("...")
print(all_incidents.tail(3))

Combined: (27, 7)
  incident_id entity_id   location        date   investigator  \
0     INC-001   ENT-001     Arkham  2024-03-12   Dr. Armitage   
1     INC-002   ENT-002    Dunwich  2024-03-15   Dr. Armitage   
2     INC-003   ENT-001  Innsmouth  2024-04-02  Agent Peaslee   

   anomaly_severity containment_status  
0                 5             Active  
1                 4            Unknown  
2                 5             Active  
...
   incident_id entity_id           location        date investigator  \
24     INC-025   ENT-004              Salem  2024-06-28  Agent Waite   
25     INC-026   ENT-005  Miskatonic Valley  2024-07-08   Dr. Morgan   
26     INC-027   ENT-007         Providence  2024-07-22  Agent Waite   

    anomaly_severity containment_status  
24                 3          Contained  
25                 2          Contained  
26                 4             Active  


Understanding pd.concat():
pd.concat() takes a list of DataFrames and stacks them along an axis. The default behavior stacks rows (axis="index"), which is what we want here.
The ignore_index=True parameter tells pandas to discard the original indexes and assign a new sequential index starting at 0. Without it, the index from north (0 through 14) would be followed by the index from south (also 0 through 11), producing duplicate index values. That is usually not what you want when combining records from different sources.
After concatenation: 15 + 12 = 27 rows, one clean sequential index from 0 to 26.


Verify the result

print(all_incidents.info())
print("\nMissing values:")
print(all_incidents.isnull().sum())

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 27 entries, 0 to 26
Data columns (total 7 columns):
 #   Column              Non-Null Count  Dtype 
---  ------              --------------  ----- 
 0   incident_id         27 non-null     object
 1   entity_id           27 non-null     object
 2   location            27 non-null     object
 3   date                27 non-null     object
 4   investigator        27 non-null     object
 5   anomaly_severity    27 non-null     int64 
 6   containment_status  27 non-null     object
dtypes: int64(1), object(6)
memory usage: 1.6+ KB
None

Missing values:
incident_id           0
entity_id             0
location              0
date                  0
investigator          0
anomaly_severity      0
containment_status    0
dtype: int64


Understanding the output:
All 27 rows are present, all columns are non-null, and the data types are what we expect. The combined DataFrame is ready to use.
pd.concat() also works along columns (axis="columns") and with more than two DataFrames. Pass any number of DataFrames in the list. Chapter 8 covers the keys parameter, which labels each row with its source, creating a hierarchical index in the result.

title: Week 12 Demo: Combining and Reshaping DataFrames
text:
Part 2: Joining DataFrames with pd.merge()

The scenario
The Bureau keeps a central registry of all known entities: their classification, origin dimension, and threat level. The incident reports reference entities by ID but do not include those details directly. To answer questions like “which classification of entity generates the most incidents?” or “what is the average threat level of entities that have been contained?”, the incident data needs to be joined to the registry.
This is a many-to-one join: many incidents can reference the same entity, but each entity appears only once in the registry.


Load the entity registry

registry = pd.read_csv("entity_registry.csv")
print(registry)

  entity_id     entity_name classification origin_dimension  threat_level
0   ENT-001         Cthulhu    Ancient One           R'lyeh            10
1   ENT-002    Nyarlathotep    Ancient One       Outer Void             9
2   ENT-003           Dagon         Herald       Deep Abyss             7
3   ENT-004        Shoggoth        Servant  Primordial Deep             6
4   ENT-005           Mi-Go  Manifestation          Yuggoth             5
5   ENT-006        Deep One        Servant       Deep Abyss             4
6   ENT-007         Byakhee         Herald       Outer Void             5
7   ENT-008      Star Spawn        Servant           R'lyeh             7
8   ENT-009     Yog-Sothoth    Ancient One   All Dimensions            10
9   ENT-010  Shub-Niggurath    Ancient One      Black Woods             9


Understanding the output:
The registry has 10 entities. Each has an entity_id that also appears in the incident reports. entity_id is the shared key that connects the two tables.


Inner join: keep only matching rows

merged_inner = pd.merge(all_incidents, registry, on="entity_id")

print(f"Incidents: {len(all_incidents)} rows")
print(f"Registry: {len(registry)} rows")
print(f"Inner join result: {len(merged_inner)} rows")
print(f"\nEntities in result: {sorted(merged_inner['entity_name'].unique())}")

Incidents: 27 rows
Registry: 10 rows
Inner join result: 27 rows

Entities in result: ['Byakhee', 'Cthulhu', 'Dagon', 'Deep One', 'Mi-Go', 'Nyarlathotep', 'Shoggoth', 'Star Spawn']


Understanding the inner join:
The on="entity_id" parameter tells pandas which column to use as the join key. An inner join keeps only rows where the key value appears in both tables. Every incident references an entity, so all 27 incident rows survive. But look at the entity count: only 8 of the 10 entities in the registry appear in the result.
To confirm which entities were dropped, compare the entity IDs in each table directly.

registry_entities = set(registry["entity_id"])
incident_entities = set(all_incidents["entity_id"])
missing = registry_entities - incident_entities
print(f"Entities in registry but not in incidents: {missing}")
print(registry[registry["entity_id"].isin(missing)][["entity_id", "entity_name"]])

Entities in registry but not in incidents: {'ENT-010', 'ENT-009'}
  entity_id     entity_name
8   ENT-009     Yog-Sothoth
9   ENT-010  Shub-Niggurath


Understanding the output:
Yog-Sothoth and Shub-Niggurath are in the registry but have no incident reports. The inner join silently dropped them. Whether that is the right behavior depends on what you are analyzing. If you want a complete picture of all entities regardless of incident history, the inner join gives you the wrong answer.


Left join: keep all rows from the left table

merged_left = pd.merge(registry, all_incidents, on="entity_id", how="left")

print(f"Left join result: {len(merged_left)} rows")
print(f"\nRows where incident_id is NaN (no incidents):")
print(merged_left[merged_left["incident_id"].isna()][["entity_id", "entity_name", "classification", "threat_level"]])

Left join result: 29 rows

Rows where incident_id is NaN (no incidents):
   entity_id     entity_name classification  threat_level
27   ENT-009     Yog-Sothoth    Ancient One            10
28   ENT-010  Shub-Niggurath    Ancient One             9


Understanding the left join:
how="left" keeps every row from the left DataFrame (the registry) regardless of whether a match exists in the right DataFrame (the incidents). When no match is found, pandas fills the incident columns with NaN.
The result has 29 rows. That is 27 matched incident rows plus 2 unmatched registry rows for Yog-Sothoth and Shub-Niggurath. These are the Ancient Ones with no recorded incidents. Their absence is now visible rather than silently dropped.
Which join is “right” depends on the question. If you want to count incidents per entity and you need the zero counts to appear, use a left join on the registry. If you only care about entities that have incidents, use an inner join on the incident table.


Right join: keep all rows from the right table

merged_right = pd.merge(registry, all_incidents, on="entity_id", how="right")

print(f"Right join result: {len(merged_right)} rows")

Right join result: 27 rows


Understanding the right join:
how="right" is the mirror of how="left". Every row from the right DataFrame is kept. Unmatched rows from the left get NaN. Here the right table is all_incidents, so all 27 incident rows appear. This produces the same result as the inner join in this dataset because every incident references a valid entity. A right join becomes meaningful when the right table has rows with no match in the left, for example if an incident referenced an entity ID not yet in the registry.


Outer join: keep all rows from both tables

merged_outer = pd.merge(registry, all_incidents, on="entity_id", how="outer")

print(f"Outer join result: {len(merged_outer)} rows")
print(f"\nRows with NaN in incident_id (entities with no incidents):")
print(merged_outer[merged_outer["incident_id"].isna()][["entity_id", "entity_name"]])

Outer join result: 29 rows

Rows with NaN in incident_id (entities with no incidents):
   entity_id     entity_name
27   ENT-009     Yog-Sothoth
28   ENT-010  Shub-Niggurath


Understanding the outer join:
how="outer" takes the union: every row from both tables appears in the result. Rows with no match on either side get NaN in the columns from the other table. This is the most inclusive option. Use it when you need a complete picture of everything in both tables.


A note on key column names
When the key column has the same name in both DataFrames, on="column_name" works. When the column names differ, use left_on= and right_on= to specify each one separately. Chapter 8 covers merging on index values using left_index=True and right_index=True.

title: Week 12 Demo: Combining and Reshaping DataFrames
text:
Part 3: Reshaping with pd.melt()

The scenario
The Bureau also tracks monthly sighting counts for each entity. The data arrived in wide format: one row per entity, with each month as its own column.

sightings = pd.read_csv("monthly_sightings.csv")
print(sightings)

  entity_id     entity_name  Jan  Feb  Mar  Apr  May  Jun
0   ENT-001         Cthulhu    4    3    7    5    8    6
1   ENT-002    Nyarlathotep    2    4    3    5    4    6
2   ENT-003           Dagon    1    2    2    3    2    4
3   ENT-004        Shoggoth    3    2    4    2    3    2
4   ENT-005           Mi-Go    2    1    3    2    4    3
5   ENT-006        Deep One    5    4    6    5    7    5
6   ENT-007         Byakhee    1    1    2    2    1    3
7   ENT-008      Star Spawn    2    3    2    4    3    4
8   ENT-009     Yog-Sothoth    0    1    0    1    0    1
9   ENT-010  Shub-Niggurath    1    0    1    0    2    1


Understanding wide format:
Wide format is easy to read as a table and intuitive for data entry, but it is difficult to work with analytically. If you want to find the highest sighting count across all entities and months, you cannot filter a single column. The data is spread across six. If you want to sort by month, there is no month column to sort on.
Long format restructures this so each row is one observation: one entity, one month, one count. That gives you a single month column and a single sighting_count column, which makes every filtering and sorting operation straightforward.


Reshape from wide to long with pd.melt()

sightings_long = pd.melt(
    sightings,
    id_vars=["entity_id", "entity_name"],
    var_name="month",
    value_name="sighting_count"
)

print(f"Wide format: {sightings.shape}")
print(f"Long format: {sightings_long.shape}")
print(sightings_long.head(12))

Wide format: (10, 8)
Long format: (60, 4)
   entity_id     entity_name month  sighting_count
0    ENT-001         Cthulhu   Jan               4
1    ENT-002    Nyarlathotep   Jan               2
2    ENT-003           Dagon   Jan               1
3    ENT-004        Shoggoth   Jan               3
4    ENT-005           Mi-Go   Jan               2
5    ENT-006        Deep One   Jan               5
6    ENT-007         Byakhee   Jan               1
7    ENT-008      Star Spawn   Jan               2
8    ENT-009     Yog-Sothoth   Jan               0
9    ENT-010  Shub-Niggurath   Jan               1
10   ENT-001         Cthulhu   Feb               3
11   ENT-002    Nyarlathotep   Feb               4


Understanding pd.melt():
pd.melt() takes a wide DataFrame and converts it to long format.

id_vars specifies the columns that stay as identifiers. These repeat for each observation. Here entity_id and entity_name identify which entity each row belongs to.
var_name gives a name to the new column that holds the former column headers. The month names (Jan, Feb, etc.) become values in a column called month.
value_name gives a name to the new column that holds the values. The sighting counts become values in a column called sighting_count.

The result has 10 entities × 6 months = 60 rows. Every observation is now a single row with a clear entity, month, and count.


Analyze the long format data

# Which entity had the highest sighting count in any single month?
top = sightings_long.sort_values("sighting_count", ascending=False).head(5)
print("Top 5 single-month sighting counts:")
print(top[["entity_name", "month", "sighting_count"]])

Top 5 single-month sighting counts:
     entity_name month  sighting_count
40       Cthulhu   May               8
45      Deep One   May               7
20       Cthulhu   Mar               7
25      Deep One   Mar               6
51  Nyarlathotep   Jun               6



# Filter to a single month
may_sightings = sightings_long[sightings_long["month"] == "May"].sort_values(
    "sighting_count", ascending=False
)
print("\nMay sightings by entity:")
print(may_sightings[["entity_name", "sighting_count"]])


May sightings by entity:
       entity_name  sighting_count
40         Cthulhu               8
45        Deep One               7
41    Nyarlathotep               4
44           Mi-Go               4
43        Shoggoth               3
47      Star Spawn               3
42           Dagon               2
49  Shub-Niggurath               2
46         Byakhee               1
48     Yog-Sothoth               0


Understanding the output:
Both of these operations would have been awkward in the wide format. In long format, they are standard single-column operations. This is why reshaping data before analysis matters. The format determines what operations are natural.

title: Week 12 Demo: Combining and Reshaping DataFrames
text:
Conclusion

What You’ve Learned
This demo introduced three tools for working with data spread across multiple sources.
pd.concat() stacks DataFrames that share the same structure. Use ignore_index=True when the row index carries no meaningful information and you want a clean sequential index in the result. Pass any number of DataFrames in a list.
pd.merge() joins two DataFrames on a shared key column, the same logic as a SQL JOIN. The how parameter controls which rows survive: "inner" keeps only matching rows, "left" keeps all rows from the left table, "right" keeps all rows from the right table, and "outer" keeps all rows from both. The choice of join type is a data question, not a technical one. It depends on what you need to know and whether missing matches should appear as NaN or be dropped entirely.
pd.melt() reshapes wide data into long format. id_vars identifies which columns stay as identifiers, var_name names the new column holding the former column headers, and value_name names the new column holding the values. Long format makes filtering, sorting, and grouping operations straightforward because each observation is a single row.
set_index() and reset_index() move columns in and out of the index. set_index() promotes a column to the index; reset_index() moves the index back into a column and restores the default integer index. They appear frequently as supporting operations alongside merges and reshapes.


What the Textbook Covers Next
Chapter 8 goes further than this demo on several topics worth reading.
Hierarchical indexing and MultiIndex: pandas supports multiple levels of row or column labels. A MultiIndex allows you to represent higher-dimensional data in a flat table. The swaplevel() and sort_index(level=) methods manipulate level order. This is the foundation for stack() and unstack().
stack() and unstack(): stack() rotates column labels into row labels, producing a Series from a DataFrame. unstack() does the reverse. These are the lower-level operations that pivot() and melt() build on.
pivot(): The inverse of melt(). Converts long format back to wide format using one column as the row index, one as the column headers, and one as the values.
combine_first(): Patches missing values in one DataFrame with values from another at matching index positions. Useful for filling gaps from a secondary source without a full merge.


Applying These Tools in Week 13
Week 13 is a cumulative application week. You will work with a multi-source dataset that requires you to combine, join, and reshape data before analysis can begin. The workflow follows the same pattern you have built across prior application weeks: assess the data, prepare it, then analyze it. The Chapter 8 tools are the preparation step for data that starts in multiple files or the wrong shape.

title: Week 13 Assignment
text:
The Situation
A regional tabletop RPG league ran two separate groups through a shared campaign setting over a six-month season. Each group kept its own encounter log. Every time the party fought a creature, someone recorded what creature it was, how many players were at the table, where the session took place, and how it turned out.
The two groups logged their data independently, so the records exist in two separate files with the same structure. The league also maintains a bestiary: a reference table that maps creature IDs to names, types, challenge ratings, and habitats. Finally, the league tracked how often each creature appeared in encounters across the six months of the season in a separate summary table.
The coordinator wants a unified picture of the season before the end-of-year recap. That means combining the two logs, enriching the combined data with creature details from the bestiary, and analyzing the monthly encounter trends. Your job is to build that picture.

title: Week 13 Assignment
text:
What You Need to Deliver

Part 1: Load and Assess
Load both encounter log files. Before doing anything else, inspect both DataFrames and document what you find: confirm that the column structure matches across both files, identify where missing values exist and how many, and flag any quality issues that will need to be addressed before analysis. Your assessment should be specific. If you find a problem, name it and describe where it appears.


Part 2: Combine and Clean
Combine both encounter logs into a single working DataFrame. Use a markdown cell to explain what the index behavior is when combining two DataFrames this way and what you did to produce a clean sequential index.
After combining, address any quality issues you identified in Part 1. For each issue you fix, use a markdown cell to explain what the problem was, what you did, and why that was the right choice. Verify the cleaned DataFrame before moving on.


Part 3: Enrich with the Bestiary
Load bestiary.csv and examine its structure. Identify the column that connects it to the combined encounter log.
Join the bestiary to the combined encounter log. Before writing the code, use a markdown cell to explain which join type you are using and why. Your explanation should address what happens to encounter records that have no matching bestiary entry, and what happens to bestiary creatures that never appear in either log. A join type selection without a written justification is not complete.
Show the shape of the result and confirm it matches your expectation.


Part 4: Analysis
With the merged DataFrame, answer the following questions for the coordinator’s season recap. All analysis must use methods from Chapters 1–8.
Encounter outcomes. How many encounters ended in each outcome (Victory, Defeat, Retreat) across both groups combined? Show counts sorted from most to least common.
Creature type breakdown. How many encounters involved each creature type? Show counts for all types that appear in the data.
Challenge rating distribution. What are the summary statistics for challenge rating across all matched encounters? Then show the five highest challenge rating creatures the groups actually encountered, sorted from highest to lowest.
Group comparison. How many encounters did each group log, and what was each group’s Victory rate as a percentage of their total encounters?


Part 5: Reshape and Analyze
Load monthly_encounters.csv and display it as-is so the wide format is visible.
Reshape the table into long format so that each row represents one creature in one month. Use a markdown cell to explain what changed between the wide and long versions and what each parameter in your reshape call controls. Show the first several rows of the result to confirm the shape is correct.
With the long-format DataFrame, answer the following:
Single-month peak. Which creature had the highest encounter count in any single month? Show that row from the long-format table.
March breakdown. Show all encounter counts for March, sorted from most to least active.
One creature across the season. Show the encounter counts for Goblin across all six months in the order they appear in the reshaped table.


Part 6: Save
Save the merged DataFrame from Part 3 to a file called encounters_merged.csv. Do not write the row index to the file.

title: Week 13 Assignment
text:
Technical Requirements
Your notebook must demonstrate the complete workflow from raw files to finished analysis. It must run successfully using Restart Kernel and Run All.
All combining, joining, and reshaping must use the Chapter 8 tools: pd.concat() for stacking the two logs, pd.merge() for the bestiary join, and pd.melt() for reshaping the monthly table. All cleaning must use the Chapter 7 methods you have already practiced. All analysis must use DataFrame operations – no raw loops for filtering or computing statistics.
Markdown cells are required at every decision point. Loading data and running code without explaining what you found and why you made the choices you made is not sufficient.
Structure your notebook so each part is clearly labeled. Make it easy to verify that every required output is present.

title: Week 13 Assignment
text:
Video Reflection
Record a short video demonstrating your understanding of this assignment.
Part 1: Walk through two decisions
Address both of the following in your video:
The merge decision. Explain which join type you chose for the bestiary merge and why. Walk through what would have been different if you had chosen a different join type. Be specific about which rows would appear or disappear and why that matters for the analysis.
The melt operation. Walk through what the monthly encounters table looked like before and after the reshape. Explain what each parameter controlled and why long format is easier to work with for the analysis questions in Part 5.
Part 2: Reflect on your learning
Choose ONE of the following:

Challenge or error: Describe a problem you ran into while completing this assignment, how you diagnosed it, and how you resolved it
Key takeaway: Explain one concept from Chapter 8 that this assignment made clearer and why it matters for working with real data

Video submission requirements:

Clear video and audio quality
Introduce yourself at the beginning (name)
Name your video file: lastname_Week13Reflection
Upload your video to Kaltura

Make sure the link text includes lastname_Week13Reflection in the link name
