title: Week 1 — VSCode Setup and First Notebook (Windows)
text:
What you’re doing
You are going to open a Jupyter notebook in VS Code and run it successfully using the course Python environment.


1) Open the course folder in VS Code

Open VS Code
Go to File → Open Folder…
Open your course work folder (example: C:\cmsc-1217\)



2) Install the extensions (if you don’t already have them)
In VS Code, install:

Python (Microsoft)
Jupyter (Microsoft)



3) Create the course Python environment and select it in VS Code (one-time)

3A) Create the course environment folder

Create a folder on your C: drive named:

C:\cmsc-1217-env\




3B) Create the virtual environment (.venv)

Open Command Prompt (Windows search → type “Command Prompt”)
Run:

cd C:\cmsc-1217-env
python -m venv .venv


3C) Activate the environment
In the same Command Prompt window, run:
C:\cmsc-1217-env\.venv\Scripts\activate.bat
You should see (.venv) appear at the start of the line.


3D) Install the notebook packages (first-time setup)
Still in the activated environment, run:
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install numpy pandas matplotlib jupyter


3E) Select the environment in VS Code (Interpreter)

In VS Code, press Ctrl + Shift + P
Type Python: Select Interpreter
Choose the interpreter that looks like:


C:\cmsc-1217-env\.venv\Scripts\python.exe

If you don’t see it:

choose Enter interpreter path…
paste: C:\cmsc-1217-env\.venv\Scripts\python.exe

This is the environment we will use all semester.



4) Open the notebook

In the Explorer panel, click the notebook file (.ipynb) your instructor provided.



5) Select the notebook kernel (first time only)
The first time you run a notebook, VS Code may ask you to pick a kernel.
Choose the kernel that matches the course environment path:

C:\cmsc-1217-env\.venv\Scripts\python.exe

This can feel repetitive the first time. That’s normal. Once selected, it usually stays set for that folder.


6) Select the kernel, then run a quick test

In the top-right of the notebook, click Select Kernel.
Choose the kernel that points to the course environment:
C:\cmsc-1217-env\.venv\Scripts\python.exe
(It may also show up as “Python 3.x (.venv)”—the key is that it points to cmsc-1217-env\.venv.)
Click + Code.
In the code block that appears, paste:

print("Notebook is running")

Run it by clicking the Run (▶) icon on the code block, or press Shift + Enter.
You should see Notebook is running appear underneath.



7) Quick confirmation (your instructor may ask for this)
Run this in a cell:
import sys
sys.executable
It should show something like:
C:\cmsc-1217-env\.venv\Scripts\python.exe


If something goes wrong

If you see ModuleNotFoundError (missing package), you are almost always using the wrong interpreter/kernel.

Re-do Step 3 and Step 5 and pick the one that points to cmsc-1217-env\.venv.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:
You will use the same core setup in both of these courses:
The difference between the courses is which environment/packages you install and which tools are emphasized. This page includes small callouts to help you follow the right parts at the right time.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:
Overview
In these courses, you will work inside a conda environment. An environment is an isolated Python installation (Python + packages) stored in a folder on your computer. This helps everyone use consistent tools and reduces setup conflicts.
You will use the following tools:

Miniconda Miniconda is a small installer that provides Conda, a system for managing Python installations and packages. Miniconda does not include many data-science packages by default; instead, it gives you a controlled way to install exactly what a course requires.
Conda (the environment manager) Conda is software that can create, activate, and manage environments. It can also install packages (libraries) in a way that keeps them consistent and reduces compatibility problems, especially for scientific packages.
Conda environment A conda environment is a self-contained folder that contains:

a specific version of Python, and
a specific set of installed packages. Each environment is separate from other environments on your computer. This means one course can use one set of packages (and versions) without affecting another course or your personal Python installation.

JupyterLab (notebook workspace) JupyterLab is a browser-based programming environment used for data analysis and machine learning. It allows you to write code in cells, run those cells, and view output (tables, plots, printed results) directly below the code. JupyterLab is used in both courses because it supports step-by-step work and makes it easier to review results.
IPython (interactive Python terminal — CMSC-1217 only) IPython is an enhanced interactive Python terminal. It provides features that support exploratory work, such as command history, tab completion, and built-in help. In CMSC-1217, IPython is used to practice interactive exploration and reinforce core Python concepts in a terminal-based workflow.

How these pieces fit together
Miniconda installs Conda, and Conda is the tool you use to create a course environment. When you activate that environment, your terminal temporarily switches so that the python command (and tools like jupyter) run from the environment’s folder. You then start Jupyter (JupyterLab or Jupyter Notebook), and the notebook runs Python from the active environment, using the packages installed there. This sequence—install Miniconda → create environment → activate environment → start Jupyter → run notebooks—is the standard workflow you will use throughout the course.

Important: During Miniconda installation, do not add Miniconda to PATH. We will use the Miniconda/Anaconda Prompt when working for these courses.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:
1) Install Miniconda

Download and install Miniconda (64-bit) for Windows. https://www.anaconda.com/download
During installation, do not add Miniconda to PATH.

Why not add to PATH: Many of you already have a Python installation from python.org. Not adding Miniconda to PATH prevents Miniconda from changing which python runs in non-course terminals.
Miniconda is a lightweight installer that provides:

Conda (a package and environment manager), and
a basic Python installation and its dependencies.

Miniconda is intentionally minimal: it does not install a large collection of data-science packages by default. Instead, you install only the packages required for the course.
Relationship to Anaconda
Miniconda and Anaconda are closely related:

Anaconda Distribution is a larger installer that comes with hundreds of packages pre-installed, plus optional tools like Anaconda Navigator.
Miniconda is a smaller installer that includes only conda, Python, and a small core set of packages, and then you add what you need.

For these courses, Miniconda is a good fit because it helps us keep your course environment clean, consistent, and easier to troubleshoot.
Why Miniconda/Conda is common in data analysis and machine learning
Data analysis and machine learning often rely on libraries that include compiled components. Conda is widely used in these areas because it can install many scientific packages as prebuilt binaries, which reduces installation difficulties (especially on Windows).
Conda also supports creating separate environments so different projects can use different versions of Python/packages without interfering with each other.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:
2) Open the conda terminal
Open Anaconda Command Prompt from the Start menu.
You should see something like:
(base) C:\Users\yourname>
Why we use this terminal
Anaconda/Miniconda Prompt is a terminal that is already configured to recognize conda commands such as conda createand conda activate. Using this prompt reduces setup errors because it starts with the correct conda settings.
What (base) means
(base) indicates that you are currently in conda’s default environment. This environment exists mainly so that conda can run and manage other environments. In this course, you will usually not do your work in (base). Instead, you will create and activate a course environment (for example, pydata-book or cmsc-2208) and work there.
How to interpret the prompt
The name in parentheses is your current environment:

(base) means you are in the default conda environment
(pydata-book) or (cmsc-2208) means you are in the course environment

A quick habit that prevents many problems: before running course commands, look at the parentheses and confirm you are in the correct environment.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:
3) Configure conda package channels
Run the following two commands, one at a time, pressing Enter after each.
First, run:
conda config --add channels conda-forge
Then, run:
conda config --set channel_priority strict
Conda downloads packages from sources called channels. A channel is a repository that hosts packaged software (similar in purpose to an “app store,” but for programming libraries). When you install a package with conda, conda searches the configured channels to find a compatible version of that package and its dependencies.
We use conda-forge because it is a widely used community-maintained channel that provides current, well-tested builds of many scientific and data-analysis libraries (for example, NumPy, pandas, matplotlib, and scikit-learn). Using a consistent channel helps ensure that most students receive the same package builds and reduces platform-specific installation problems.
Setting strict channel priority means conda will prefer packages from the highest-priority channel (in this case, conda-forge) rather than mixing packages from multiple channels. This matters because mixing package sources can sometimes lead to version conflicts or dependency mismatches. Strict priority reduces those problems by keeping package selection consistent.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:
4) Create the course environment
You will create one conda environment for the course you are taking currently.
What an environment is:
In programming, an environment is the set of software used to run your code, such as:

the language runtime (for us, Python),
installed libraries/packages,
and related tools (such as Jupyter).

Many languages and toolchains support environments (for example, Python can use virtual environments, and other ecosystems have their own dependency managers). The goal is the same: keep project dependencies organized and consistent.
What a conda environment is:
In this course we use a conda environment, which is a self-contained folder on your computer that includes:

a specific version of Python, and
a specific set of installed packages.

We use conda because it provides a consistent way to install scientific and data-analysis libraries (especially on Windows) and makes it straightforward to reproduce the same setup across different computers.
Do not create both environments.
If you are in CMSC-1217 Introduction to Data Analytics
Run this command (then press Enter):
conda create -n pydata-book python=3.11
If prompted, type y.
This creates an environment named pydata-book. The name is just a label for this course’s Python setup.
If you are in CMSC-2208 Introduction to Machine Learning
Run this command (then press Enter):
conda create -n cmsc-2208 python=3.11
If prompted, type y.
This creates an environment named cmsc-2208. The name is just a label for this course’s Python setup.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:
5) Activate the environment (choose your course)
Before installing packages or starting JupyterLab, you must activate your course environment.
What “activate” means: It tells the terminal to use the Python and packages inside your course environment. When the environment is active, commands like python and jupyter will run from the correct course setup.
You will activate one environment: the one for the course you are taking now.

If you are in CMSC-1217 Introduction to Data Analytics
Run:
conda activate pydata-book


If you are in CMSC-2208 Introduction to Machine Learning
Run:
conda activate cmsc-2208
After you activate, your prompt will begin with the environment name, for example:

(pydata-book) ...
(cmsc-2208) ...

Rule: Before you do course work, confirm that the prompt shows your course environment name.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:
6) Install packages (inside the active environment)

A) CMSC-1217 packages
With (pydata-book) active, run:
conda install -c conda-forge numpy pandas=2.0.3 matplotlib jupyterlab notebook ipykernel
This installs:

numpy: arrays and numerical computing
pandas: data tables (DataFrames)
matplotlib: plotting
jupyterlab: notebook interface
ipykernel: the component that allows this environment to run notebooks as a Jupyter kernel


CMSC-1217 only: You will also use IPython in this course. IPython will work once the environment is active.



B) CMSC-2208 packages (required)
With (cmsc-2208) active, run:
conda install -c conda-forge numpy pandas matplotlib jupyterlab notebook ipykernel scikit-learn scipy
This installs the data stack plus the machine learning libraries used in CMSC-2208.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:
7) Verify your installation (imports + version confirmation)
Run this command in the active environment:
python -c "import sys; import numpy as np; import pandas as pd; import matplotlib; print('python', sys.version.split()[0]); print('numpy', np.__version__); print('pandas', pd.__version__); print('matplotlib', matplotlib.__version__)"
What this step is doing (and why it can take a moment):
This command does two things:

Imports the core libraries (numpy, pandas, matplotlib). Importing is the real verification step. It confirms the packages are installed correctly in this environment and that Python can load them without errors.
Prints version numbers after the imports succeed. This helps confirm you are using the expected tool versions for the course.

Because these libraries are large and include compiled components, the first successful import in a new environment may take longer than you expect. That is normal.

CMSC-2208 only
For CMSC-2208, also verify:
python -c "import sklearn, scipy; print('sklearn', sklearn.__version__); print('scipy', scipy.__version__)"
This confirms that the machine learning libraries required for the course can be imported correctly in the CMSC-2208 environment.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
section: 8) Create the course folder structure (choose your course)
text:

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:

git --version
If Git is not installed, install Git for Windows, then re-check git --version.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
section: 10) Download the book companion files (choose your course)
text:

section: 11) Start Jupyter Notebook in the correct folder (choose yourcourse)
text:

A browser tab will open showing the contents of your course-work folder.

title: Shared Setup (Windows) — Miniconda, Course Environments, IPython, and JupyterLab
text:
Common issues and solutions

Issue: Imports fail inside a notebook
Most common cause: the wrong environment/kernel is active.
Solution

In the notebook, run sys.executable to confirm the environment path.
If it is incorrect, switch the notebook kernel to the correct one:

Kernel → Change Kernel (select the environment for your course)




Issue: JupyterLab opens in the wrong folder
Solution: cd into the correct course-work folder before starting JupyterLab.


Issue: You forgot to activate the environment
Solution: close JupyterLab (Ctrl+C), then re-run the launch commands in Section 11.

title: General Course Policies
text:
Academic integrity

Students must maintain academic integrity (no cheating, plagiarism, unauthorized materials, falsification, etc.).
Violations follow SCTCC Academic Integrity Policy/Procedure and may be recorded; disciplinary action may apply.
Academic integrity violations result in a grade reduction.



Professional conduct and behavior

Online discussions must be civil, respectful, and relevant.
Disruptive behavior on any course communication platform is not tolerated; may result in grade penalties and/or removal per the Code of Student Conduct.
Respect for differing viewpoints is required; maintain professional language and conduct.
If disruptive behavior causes a student to miss an assignment, they receive no credit for that assignment.
If sanctioned for disruptive behavior, students waive the right to a warning on a second occurrence.
Substance policy for online sessions: inebriation/being under the influence, displaying alcohol, or drug paraphernalia results in removal from the session and/or course; no smoking during online school activites.



Student privacy / third-party communication

Course progress is discussed only with the student, not parents/guardians/third parties, unless an Information Release is on file.
If a third party contacts the instructor, the instructor will defer communication to the student.
Students may not invite third parties to attend any online activity (office hours, group meetings).
Requests to release progress information to a third party should go through the Dean’s office.



Attendance / participation

Attendance is expected and monitored; students are responsible for tracking absences.
Attendance is determined by D2L logins and weekly assignment submissions.
Not logging in and/or not submitting work for a week counts as an absence (including weeks without due assignments unless it’s a scheduled holiday/break).
Students must log in and complete an activity by 11:30 PM on the first Wednesday of week 1 or they may be dropped.
Missing two consecutive weeks → dropped.
Missing two total weeks → one-letter-grade reduction (10% of total points).
Missing three total weeks → an additional one-letter-grade reduction.
Additional absences beyond three → additional letter-grade reduction per occurrence.
Attendance is handled per class (one class doesn’t cover another).



Technology and platform expectations

Students are responsible for having required computer/technology for the course.
Students are responsible for learning and using D2L (primary channel for schedules, grades, and announcements).



Email communication

Email is the primary method to contact the instructor; students must use their college email account.
Emails must include a subject line with course name and section/class time.
Students are expected to check email daily and read instructor messages/attachments.
On nights homework is due, only administrative email items will be answered; technical homework questions will not be addressed.
Response time: by end of next business day; Friday emails answered Monday.

title: Week 2 Guide
text:
This week focuses on learning the core workflow you’ll repeat throughout data analytics: starting with a file, converting it into structured records, and producing summaries that answer questions.
In the Week 2 demo, you practice the pipeline end-to-end:

create or obtain a small CSV-style dataset
inspect what the file contains
parse each row into fields and convert types
validate rows and handle errors safely with try/except
build a clean in-memory dataset you can analyze
compute unique values, frequency counts, rankings, and summary statistics
compare results across subsets (success vs error)
see a streaming approach using a generator (yield) for large files

title: Week 2 Guide
text:
This week focuses on learning the core workflow you’ll repeat throughout data analytics: starting with a file, converting it into structured records, and producing summaries that answer questions.
In the Week 2 demo, you practice the pipeline end-to-end:

create or obtain a small CSV-style dataset
inspect what the file contains
parse each row into fields and convert types
validate rows and handle errors safely with try/except
build a clean in-memory dataset you can analyze
compute unique values, frequency counts, rankings, and summary statistics
compare results across subsets (success vs error)
see a streaming approach using a generator (yield) for large files

title: Week 2 Guide
text:
Chapter 3 concepts used this week

Files and paths
Chapter 3 reviews file input/output using open(...) and the standard read/write patterns. You’ll use these skills to create a data file, read it back, and confirm that the contents are what you expect before you try to analyze anything.


CSV file and records
The demo uses a CSV-file structure: a header row followed by data rows. A key analytics habit is thinking in terms of records:

one row represents one record
the header describes what each field means
the goal is to turn each record into something Python can work with by name



Parsing and validation
Parsing is the bridge between “a line of text” and “a record we can analyze.” In the demo, parsing includes:

splitting a line into fields
stripping whitespace so fields are consistent
converting numeric fields to integers so we can compute and compare
checking that the row has the expected shape (the expected number of fields)



Exceptions as workflow control
In data work, a single bad record shouldn’t stop the entire process. This week emphasizes a standard pattern:

try to parse a row
if it works, keep it
if it fails, record the failure and keep going



Core data structures for analytics
This week is intentionally standard Python” data work, using standard structures in a data context:

list: storing many records (your cleaned dataset)
dictionary: representing one record (field names → values)
set: getting unique values (distinct users/endpoints)
dictionary counting with .get(): building frequency tables
tuples from .items(): working with (key, value) pairs for ranking and sorting



Lambda
Sometimes you need a small function only once—usually to plug into something like sorted(..., key=...) or max(..., key=...). Writing a full def function for that can feel heavy when the logic is simple.
lambda is Python’s way to write a small anonymous function in a single line. It’s not a different kind of function—it’s still a function. The difference is that it has no name and is typically used inline when you need a quick “extract this value” rule.
The important learning goal this week is understanding what role the lambda is playing:

it takes one item at a time
it returns the value Python should use for comparison



Sorting, selecting, and key= (functions as inputs)
Chapter 3 reinforces that in Python, functions are tools you can pass into other functions. Some built-in functions don’t just work on simple numbers or strings—they work on more complex items like tuples or dictionary records. In those cases, Python needs help deciding what value it should compare.
That is what the key= parameter is for. A key function takes one item and returns the value that should be used for comparison. You’ll see this idea in more than one form this week:

sorted(..., key=...) uses the key function to decide how to order items.
max(..., key=...) uses the key function to decide what “largest” means, even if the items are not plain numbers.



Generators as a scaling pattern
The generator section introduces yield and the streaming mindset:

lists are convenient when data is small
generators help when files are large because they produce one record at a time during iteration
you trade convenience (random access, reusing lists) for memory efficiency

As you work through Chapter 3, keep connecting the chapter skills to the demo workflow:

file I/O is how the data gets from disk into Python
parsing is how a text row becomes a structured record
exceptions let you keep processing even when some rows are invalid
core data structures let you answer real questions: “how many?”, “how often?”, “which is most common?”, “what changes between groups?”
generators introduce what changes when the dataset is too large to load all at once

title: Week 2 Guide
text:
Reading expectations for Week 2
As you read and run the demo, check whether you can explain the following in your own words:

What is the difference between the header row and the data rows in a CSV-style file?
What does parsing mean?
What kinds of problems make a row invalid for the demo dataset?
Why do we use try/except while processing rows instead of letting the program crash?
When would you use a set vs a dictionary in this workflow?
What does key= do in sorted(...), and what is the lambda returning?
What does yield change about how rows are processed, and what are the tradeoffs?

title: Week 2 Guide
text:
Week 2 tasks

Watch the Week 2 demo overview in your cmsc-1217 YouTube playsist.



Here is the link to the Week 2 demo.
 Download Jupyter Notebook 


Read Chapter 3 (complete).

title: Week 2 Guide
text:
Knowledge goals for Week 2
By the end of the week, you should be able to:

explain what a “record” is in a row-based dataset and why headers matter
parse a CSV file into a dictionary with consistent keys and correct types
use try/except to skip invalid rows while keeping track of errors
build and analyze a list of dictionary records
use sets for distinct values and dictionaries for frequency counts
rank results using sorting with key= (and explain what the lambda returns)
explain what a generator is and when streaming is the better approach

title: Week 4 Guide
text:
Week 4 is a cumulative application of everything from Chapters 1–4. You will work with industrial sensor data that contains errors, requiring you to apply both Week 2 parsing skills (file I/O, error handling, data structures) and Week 3 NumPy skills (arrays, vectorization, boolean indexing, statistics). The assignment presents a scenario-based problem where you determine the approach and build the complete workflow from raw data to analytical results.
Assignment:

title: Week 4 Guide
text:
Concepts applied this week

File I/O and parsing (Chapter 3)
Read a CSV file and parse each row into structured records. The file contains errors (missing fields, invalid values), so you must use try/except to handle these gracefully without crashing the analysis.


Data structures (Chapter 3)
Use lists to store cleaned records. Use dictionaries to represent each record with field names. Use sets to find distinct values (sensors in the dataset). Track errors so you can report on data quality.


NumPy arrays and vectorization (Chapter 4)
Extract numeric measurements from clean data and convert to NumPy arrays. Use vectorized operations to compute statistics (mean, standard deviation, min, max) without writing loops.


Boolean indexing (Chapter 4)
Create boolean masks by applying conditions to arrays (temperatures above a threshold, multiple conditions combined). Use masks to filter data and compute statistics on subsets.


Sorting and array methods (Chapter 4)
Use sorting methods to identify extreme values. Use np.argsort() to find positions of sorted elements. Save processed arrays in .npy format for efficient storage.

title: Week 4 Guide
text:
Week 4 tasks

Complete the Week 4 Assignment (industrial sensor analysis).
Record and submit your video reflection.

title: Week 4 Guide
text:
Knowledge goals for Week 4
By the end of Week 4, you should be able to:

parse a CSV file with error handling and report data quality metrics
convert cleaned data from Python structures to NumPy arrays
apply boolean indexing to filter arrays based on conditions
compute summary statistics on array data
use sorting methods to identify extreme values and their positions
explain the complete workflow from raw file to analytical results

title: Week 5 Guide
text:
Week 4 brought together the parsing skills from Week 2 and the NumPy operations from Week 3, building complete analysis pipelines from raw data to results. You worked with NumPy arrays for efficient computation, but managing data by position alone—remembering that “column 0 is temperature” or “row 3 is sensor SENS_004”—becomes fragile as datasets grow more complex. This week introduces pandas, Python’s primary library for working with labeled, tabular data.
pandas builds on top of NumPy arrays—it uses them internally for storage—but adds powerful features specifically designed for real-world tabular data. Instead of tracking data by position, pandas lets you label rows and columns with meaningful names. Instead of requiring all data to be the same type, pandas allows different columns to hold different types. And instead of relying on you to keep arrays aligned manually, pandas automatically aligns data by labels when combining datasets. These features make data self-documenting and prevent the alignment errors that occur with position-based indexing.
This week focuses on pandas’s two core data structures: Series (one-dimensional labeled arrays) and DataFrames (two-dimensional labeled tables). You will learn how labels enable selection by meaningful names instead of positions, how pandas automatically aligns data during operations, and how the concepts you learned in Week 3—vectorization, boolean indexing, and the axis parameter—transfer directly to pandas. Understanding these fundamentals gives you the foundation for all data manipulation work in Python.
Week 5 Demo:

title: Week 5 Guide
text:
Chapter 5 concepts used this week

What pandas adds to NumPy
NumPy arrays (Week 3) are powerful for numerical computation, but they have limitations when working with real-world tabular data. Arrays have no built-in way to label rows and columns, forcing you to track what each position represents mentally or in separate documentation. All elements must be the same type, which doesn’t match how real datasets mix numbers, text, and dates. And when combining arrays, you must manually ensure they align correctly—a single misalignment produces wrong results without warning.
pandas solves these problems by adding labels to data structures. Every row and column can have a meaningful name, making data self-documenting. Different columns can hold different types—one column of numbers, another of text, another of dates. And when combining datasets, pandas automatically aligns by labels, preventing the misalignment errors common with position-based indexing. These features transform data work from fragile position-tracking to robust label-based operations.
pandas is built on top of NumPy arrays—it uses them internally for storage—so you still get NumPy’s computational speed. But you also get the usability features needed for real-world data analysis: meaningful names, mixed types, and automatic alignment. This combination makes pandas the standard tool for data manipulation in Python.


Series: labeled one-dimensional data
A Series is pandas’s one-dimensional data structure—conceptually, it’s a NumPy array with an index attached. The index provides a label for each value, transforming data from position-based (where you must remember “element 0 is Monday”) to label-based (where the data tells you “this is Monday’s value”). This seemingly simple addition enables powerful features: you can select data by meaningful names instead of positions, combine Series and have pandas automatically match by label, and create self-documenting code where operations read like natural descriptions of what they do.
The index can be integers (the default, starting at 0 like list positions) or any immutable type—strings, dates, categorical values. Regardless of type, the index’s purpose is the same: attach meaningful labels to data so operations align correctly and code remains readable as datasets evolve.
You can create Series from various sources—lists, dictionaries, arrays—but the key concept is that every value has an associated label. When you create a Series from a dictionary, the dictionary keys automatically become the index, making this a natural way to build labeled data. The .values attribute gives you the underlying NumPy array when you need it, showing the direct connection between pandas and NumPy.


DataFrames: labeled two-dimensional data
A DataFrame is pandas’s two-dimensional data structure—a table where both rows and columns have labels. You can think of it as a spreadsheet or database table, but with the computational power of NumPy underneath. Each column is a Series, meaning columns can have different data types—one column of integers, another of floats, another of text. This matches how real-world data actually looks: a dataset of people might have numeric ages, text names, and boolean flags all in the same table.
Both dimensions have labels: the index labels rows, and column names label columns. This makes DataFrames self-documenting—instead of remembering “column 0 is temperature,” you reference the temperature column by name. The structure also connects to Week 2’s lists of dictionaries: if you had built a list where each dictionary represents one record, you can convert that directly to a DataFrame with pd.DataFrame(), with dictionary keys becoming column names.
Understanding that each column is a Series helps explain many DataFrame operations. When you select a single column, you get a Series. When you select multiple columns, you get a DataFrame. Statistical operations can work on entire DataFrames or on individual columns (Series). This consistent structure—DataFrames contain Series—makes the library easier to learn once you understand the relationship.


The index: pandas’s label system
The index is fundamental to how pandas works. It’s not just row numbers—it’s a label system that identifies what each row represents. A default integer index (0, 1, 2…) works like list positions, but the index’s real power appears when labels are meaningful: student names, dates, sensor IDs, product codes. These labels make data self-documenting and enable pandas’s most powerful feature: automatic alignment.
When you combine DataFrames or Series, pandas matches by index labels, not positions. If you add sales data for Monday through Friday to costs data for Wednesday through Sunday, pandas automatically aligns the overlapping days and marks mismatches as missing data. This prevents the silent errors that occur with position-based alignment—where adding misaligned arrays produces wrong answers without warning.
You can set the index when creating a DataFrame or change it later using .set_index(). This method takes a column name and promotes that column to become the index, removing it from the regular columns. For example, if you have a DataFrame with a “student” column, calling .set_index('student') makes student names the row labels, turning position-based row access into name-based row access.


Selection: labels versus positions
pandas provides two selection methods because you sometimes select by what you know (labels) and sometimes by where things are (positions). Label-based selection with .loc uses the names you can see: df.loc['Alice', 'quiz1'] selects Alice’s quiz1 score using meaningful identifiers. Position-based selection with .iloc uses numeric positions: df.iloc[0, 2] selects the first row, third column, regardless of labels. This works like NumPy indexing from Week 3.
Why both? Sometimes you know what you want (“get Alice’s data”) but don’t know what position that is. Other times you know the position (“get the last 10 rows”) but don’t care about labels. Having both methods lets you choose the most natural approach for each situation.
For columns, single brackets return a Series: df['temperature'] gives you the temperature column. Double brackets return a DataFrame: df[['temperature', 'humidity']] gives you a DataFrame with those two columns. This distinction matters because Series and DataFrames have different methods and behaviors. Understanding when you get each type helps you predict what operations are available.
Boolean indexing—filtering rows based on conditions—works with both label-based and position-based thinking. You create a boolean mask with a comparison (df['temperature'] > 80), then use it to filter rows. The mask itself uses position (every row gets True or False), but the result preserves labels, making filtered data self-documenting.


Automatic alignment by labels
Automatic alignment is pandas’s superpower and what makes it robust for real-world data work. When you combine DataFrames or Series with different indices, pandas matches by labels and marks non-matching positions as NaN (Not a Number). If you add Monday-Friday sales to Wednesday-Sunday costs, pandas aligns the three overlapping days, marks Monday-Tuesday as missing in costs, and marks Saturday-Sunday as missing in sales.
This prevents the silent errors common with position-based operations. In NumPy (Week 3), adding mismatched arrays either errors (different lengths) or produces wrong results (same length, wrong alignment). pandas instead makes misalignment explicit through NaN, forcing you to decide how to handle it.
This matters because real datasets rarely align perfectly—different time periods, different sensors active, different products in stock. Automatic alignment handles this complexity gracefully instead of silently producing incorrect results. When you see NaN in results, it’s telling you “these labels didn’t match”—information you need to handle the mismatch appropriately rather than unknowingly computing with misaligned data.
The alignment happens automatically in arithmetic operations, statistical computations, and data combination operations. You don’t need to manually check that indices match or write code to align them. pandas does this for you, using the index labels to ensure operations combine the correct values.


Boolean indexing and filtering
Boolean indexing from Week 3 transfers directly to pandas with one important enhancement: labels are preserved. You create a boolean mask by comparing values (df['temperature'] > 80), producing True or False for each row, then use that mask to filter (df[df['temperature'] > 80]). The syntax is identical to NumPy, and combining conditions works the same way: & for “and”, | for “or”, ~ for “not”, with parentheses around each condition.
The key difference from NumPy: when you filter a DataFrame, the row labels travel with the filtered data. If you filter for temperatures above 80 and the result includes sensors SENS_001 and SENS_004, the index tells you which sensors those are. With NumPy arrays, you’d need to track this separately. This makes pandas filtering more robust—filtered data remains self-documenting.
Multiple conditions combine using the same operators from Week 3. To find readings where temperature exceeds 200 AND humidity is below 45, you write (df['temperature'] > 200) & (df['humidity'] < 45). Each condition must be in parentheses because of operator precedence. This creates a boolean mask where only rows meeting both conditions are True, then using that mask as a filter selects those rows with their labels intact.


Operations and statistics on labeled data
The vectorized operations and statistical methods from Week 3 transfer directly to pandas with an important enhancement: results preserve labels. Computing df['temperature'] + 10 works just like NumPy array operations—no loops needed—but the result is a labeled Series that tells you which values correspond to which rows. The axis parameter works identically: df.mean(axis=0) computes down columns (one mean per column), while axis=1 computes across rows (one mean per row).
This label preservation makes results self-documenting. When you compute statistics, you don’t just get numbers—you get labeled results that tell you what each number represents. The .describe() method computes multiple statistics at once (count, mean, standard deviation, quartiles, min, max) and returns them as a labeled DataFrame, making it easy to understand summary statistics for each column.
Creating new columns is as simple as assigning to a new column name: df['total'] = df['quiz1'] + df['quiz2']. The right side is evaluated (adding two Series, which aligns by index), and the result is stored as a new column in the DataFrame. The automatic alignment ensures each row’s total corresponds to that row’s quiz scores, even if you later reorder or filter the DataFrame.
Sorting works on labeled data with .sort_values(), which sorts the entire DataFrame by one or more columns while keeping rows intact. When you sort by a column, each row moves as a complete unit—all its column values stay together. The sorted result maintains the relationship between all columns, with the index labels moving with their rows.

title: Week 6 Guide
text:
Week 6 is a cumulative application of everything from Chapters 1–5. You will work with a brewing log that contains corrupted records, requiring you to apply Week 2 parsing skills (file I/O, error handling, data structures) and Week 5 pandas skills (DataFrames, boolean indexing, column operations, statistics, and automatic alignment). The assignment presents a scenario-based problem where you determine the approach and build the complete workflow from raw data to analytical results. The key difference from Week 4 is where you end up after parsing: instead of converting measurements to NumPy arrays, you will convert your cleaned records into a pandas DataFrame and use DataFrame operations for the entire analysis.
Assignment:

title: Week 6 Guide
text:
Concepts applied this week

File I/O and parsing (Chapter 3)
Read the CSV file and parse each row into a dictionary with field names and type-converted values. This is the same parsing workflow you built in Week 2 and applied in Week 4: split each line into fields, strip whitespace, convert numeric fields to the correct type, and validate that the row has the expected structure. The file contains corrupted records from an unreliable logging system, so you must use try/except to handle errors gracefully. Track both successful parses and errors so you can report on data quality—how many records were usable and how many were not.


DataFrame creation (Chapter 5)
Once you have a list of parsed dictionaries, convert it to a pandas DataFrame using pd.DataFrame(). This is the bridge between the Week 2 parsing workflow and pandas analysis—the same bridge you would have used in Week 4 if pandas had been available. Each dictionary becomes a row, and the dictionary keys become column names. After creating the DataFrame, verify the result with .shape and .head() to confirm the data looks correct before proceeding to analysis. From this point forward, all your analysis uses DataFrame operations rather than raw loops or NumPy arrays.


Selection and boolean indexing (Chapters 4–5)
Boolean indexing transfers directly from NumPy (Week 3) to pandas, with the added benefit that filtered results preserve row labels. You apply conditions to DataFrame columns by name, and each condition produces a boolean Series. Combining conditions with & creates a multi-condition mask, just as you did with NumPy arrays in Week 4. The filtered DataFrame contains only the matching rows, with all columns and labels intact. Use .loc or .iloc when you need to select specific rows or columns by label or position rather than by condition.


Column operations and statistics (Chapter 5)
Create new columns from calculations on existing columns. For example, computing an efficiency measure by dividing one column by another produces a new Series aligned by index, which you assign as a new DataFrame column. Use .describe() for comprehensive summary statistics, individual methods like .mean() for targeted computations, and .sort_values() to rank results. All operations preserve the label structure—when you sort by a column, each row moves as a complete unit with all its values and its index label intact. This makes it straightforward to identify which specific records rank highest or lowest.


Automatic alignment (Chapter 5)
Build Series with meaningful indices and perform arithmetic between them. When you compute each apprentice’s average potency per potion name, the potion names become the index of each Series. Subtracting one Series from the other triggers pandas’s automatic alignment: labels that exist in both Series produce numeric results, while labels that appear in only one Series produce NaN. Understanding what NaN means in this context—that a particular potion was brewed by one apprentice but not the other—is essential for interpreting the comparison correctly. This is the core Week 5 concept applied to a real analytical question.

title: Week 7 Guide
text:
Since Week 2, every data pipeline you’ve built has started the same way: open a file, loop through lines, split on commas, strip whitespace, convert types, and handle errors with try/except. That manual parsing pipeline taught you what data loading actually involves, and by Week 6 you were applying it fluently to messy, real-world files. This week, you’ll see that pd.read_csv() automates that entire process in a single function call. The manual experience you’ve built makes the automation meaningful rather than magical: when pd.read_csv() detects a header row or infers column types, you understand what it’s doing because you’ve done each of those steps by hand.
Chapter 6 covers how pandas reads and writes data in multiple formats. This week’s demo focuses on the three most common scenarios: CSV files with pd.read_csv(), JSON data with Python’s json module and pd.read_json(), and live data from web APIs using the requests library. The textbook continues with HTML tables, Excel files, binary formats (HDF5, Parquet), and database interaction, all of which follow the same pd.read_*() pattern once you understand how pd.read_csv() works.
There is no walkthrough video for this week’s demo. The data loading workflow follows the same structure across formats: call a loading function, get a DataFrame, inspect the result. A section-by-section video would repeat that pattern without adding new insight. The demo is written to be self-contained. Work through it cell by cell, reading the setup text before each code block and the explanation that follows.
Week 7 Demo:

title: Week 7 Guide
text:
Chapter 6 concepts used this week

Data loading as automation of manual parsing
pd.read_csv() is not a different approach to reading CSV files. It automates the same steps you’ve been writing by hand: opening the file, detecting whether the first row is a header, splitting each line into fields, stripping whitespace, converting values to appropriate types, and assembling the result into a structured format. The difference is that pd.read_csv() returns a DataFrame instead of a list of dictionaries, and it handles these steps internally rather than requiring you to write each one.
This connection matters because it means your manual parsing knowledge transfers directly. When pd.read_csv() produces unexpected results, whether columns are the wrong type, values you expected are missing, or the header row was misidentified, you can reason about what went wrong because you understand each step it performs. The function is doing the same work your manual code did; it just does it in compiled, optimized code rather than in a Python for loop.


Type inference
When pd.read_csv() loads a file, it examines the values in each column and chooses an appropriate data type. Columns of whole numbers become int64, columns of decimal numbers become float64, and columns of text become object (or str depending on your pandas version). The textbook calls this type inference: pandas decides what type each column should be based on the actual values it finds.
Type inference usually works well, but it has a limitation that connects directly to your Week 2 experience. If a numeric column contains even one non-numeric value, like a placeholder string or a corrupted record, pandas treats the entire column as text. In your manual parsing code, you handled this with try/except and explicit type conversion. With pd.read_csv(), the solution is the na_values parameter, which tells pandas to treat specific strings as missing data during loading rather than treating them as text that forces the column to a string type.


Customizing loading behavior with parameters
The basic pd.read_csv('filename.csv') call works for well-formatted files with a header row and comma delimiters. Real-world files are not always that clean, and pd.read_csv() provides parameters to handle the variations. The textbook notes that the function has roughly 50 parameters; you don’t need to memorize them, but the ones covered this week address the most common situations.
header=None tells pandas that the file has no header row, so pandas assigns integer column names (0, 1, 2…) instead of misinterpreting the first data row as column names. names provides your own column names when the file lacks a header. index_col promotes a column to the row index during loading, and usecols loads only specific columns, which is useful when a file contains more data than you need. nrows limits how many rows are loaded, letting you inspect a large file without reading the whole thing.
These parameters solve problems you encountered in manual parsing. When you wrote code to skip a header row, to name your dictionary keys, or to select specific fields, you were doing manually what these parameters handle automatically.


Missing data and sentinel values
Real datasets use various placeholders for missing information: blank fields, “N/A”, “UNKNOWN”, “—”, or domain-specific codes. pd.read_csv() recognizes some of these by default, but custom placeholders require the na_values parameter. When you specify na_values=['UNKNOWN', '---'], pandas treats those strings as missing data and replaces them with NaN (Not a Number) during loading.
This has a consequence for data types. Because NaN is a floating-point value, any integer column that contains missing data becomes float64 to accommodate it. A column of crew sizes (150, 45, 12) that has one missing value becomes 150.0, 45.0, 12.0, NaN. This is a pandas design constraint, not an error: the integer type has no representation for “missing,” so the column must widen to a type that does.
Understanding this behavior helps you interpret your data correctly. When you see unexpected float values in what should be an integer column, it usually means missing data is present. The .info() method (from Week 5) shows non-null counts per column, making it easy to identify where missing values exist.


Writing data back out
After loading, transforming, or analyzing data, you often need to save the results. The to_csv() method writes a DataFrame to a CSV file, and its default behavior includes writing the row index as the first column. If your index is just the default integers (0, 1, 2…), that column adds no useful information to the output file. Setting index=False omits it, producing a cleaner file that matches the format most tools expect.
This pattern, loading data, processing it, and writing the result, is the standard workflow for data analysis scripts. Each pd.read_*() function has a corresponding to_*() method for writing, so the approach generalizes beyond CSV.


JSON: a flexible data format
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a text-based format built from two structures: objects (key-value pairs, equivalent to Python dictionaries) and arrays (ordered sequences, equivalent to Python lists). These structures can nest inside each other, giving JSON more flexibility than CSV’s flat tabular format. A JSON dataset can represent a planet with a list of moons, each moon having its own properties. CSV would require flattening that structure into a single table.
JSON connects directly to data structures you already know. A JSON array of objects is exactly the “list of dictionaries” pattern from Week 2, written as text. Python’s built-in json module converts between JSON strings and Python objects: json.loads() parses a JSON string into dictionaries and lists, and json.dumps() converts Python objects back to a JSON string.
For tabular JSON data (a list of objects with consistent keys), pd.read_json() can convert directly to a DataFrame. For nested or complex JSON, the json module gives you more control: you parse the JSON into Python objects, extract the parts you need, and then pass them to pd.DataFrame(). Knowing when to use each approach is a practical skill: simple flat data works with pd.read_json(), while anything nested or partially structured benefits from the manual json.loads() approach.


Web APIs: data from the internet
A web API (Application Programming Interface) is a URL that returns structured data instead of a web page. When your browser visits a URL, it receives HTML for display. When your Python code requests data from an API endpoint, it typically receives JSON. This is how applications share data programmatically: weather services, financial platforms, social media sites, and code repositories all provide APIs.
The requests library handles the HTTP communication. requests.get(url) sends a request and returns a response object. resp.raise_for_status() checks whether the request succeeded (raising an exception if it failed), and resp.json() parses the JSON response body into Python objects. From there, the workflow is familiar: you have a list of dictionaries, and pd.DataFrame() converts it to a DataFrame. The result is a clean, labeled table created from live data on the internet.
Practical considerations include rate limits (most APIs restrict how many requests you can make per hour), authentication (many APIs require an API key), and response structure (not all APIs return simple lists of dictionaries). The demo covers a public API that requires no authentication, but the general pattern, send a request, check the response, parse the JSON, convert to a DataFrame, applies to any API.


The pd.read_*() pattern
Chapter 6’s most important takeaway is that all pandas loading functions follow the same structure: call pd.read_*() with a data source, get back a DataFrame. pd.read_csv() reads comma-separated files, pd.read_json() reads JSON, pd.read_excel() reads spreadsheets, pd.read_html() extracts tables from web pages, and pd.read_sql() runs database queries. Each function has parameters specific to its format, but the result is always a DataFrame ready for the selection, filtering, and statistical operations you learned in Week 5.
The textbook also covers binary formats: Pickle (Python’s built-in serialization), HDF5 (hierarchical format for large scientific datasets), and Parquet (columnar format common in industry analytics). These are more efficient than text formats for large datasets, but the loading pattern is the same. Understanding pd.read_csv() thoroughly, as this week’s demo covers, prepares you for any of them.

title: Week 8 Guide
text:
Week 8 is a cumulative application of everything from Chapters 1–6. You will work with sales and catalog data from a board game store, requiring you to apply Week 7 data loading skills (pd.read_csv() with parameters, JSON parsing with json.loads()) and Week 5 pandas skills (DataFrames, boolean indexing, column operations, statistics). The assignment presents a scenario-based problem where you determine the approach and build the complete workflow from raw files to analytical results. The key difference from Weeks 4 and 6 is how you get data into your notebook: instead of manually parsing CSV files with open/split/strip/convert/try-except, you use pd.read_csv() to load directly into a DataFrame. The manual parsing experience from earlier weeks is what makes this meaningful — you understand what the function is doing because you have done each of those steps by hand.
Assignment:

title: Week 8 Guide
text:
Concepts applied this week

Data loading and output (Chapter 6)
pd.read_csv() automates the entire parsing pipeline you built by hand in Weeks 2, 4, and 6: opening the file, detecting the header row, splitting each line into fields, stripping whitespace, converting values to appropriate types, and assembling the result into a DataFrame. The function provides parameters to customize this behavior when files are not perfectly clean. In this assignment, the CSV file contains placeholder values that pandas does not recognize as missing by default, so you will need to determine which parameter addresses this and apply it during loading. After loading, you verify the result with .info() and .dtypes to confirm that columns have the expected types and to identify where missing values exist. When your analysis is complete, to_csv() writes a DataFrame back to a CSV file. Setting index=False omits the default row index from the output, producing a cleaner file.


Missing data and type inference (Chapter 6)
When pd.read_csv() loads a file, it infers the data type of each column based on the values it finds. If a numeric column contains even one non-numeric placeholder (like a string the system used instead of leaving the field blank), pandas treats the entire column as text. Telling pd.read_csv() which placeholder values to treat as missing causes pandas to replace them with NaN during loading, which allows the column to load as a numeric type. Because NaN is a floating-point value, integer columns that contain missing data become float64 — this is a pandas design constraint, not an error. Recognizing this pattern (unexpected float types, non-null counts that don’t match the row count) is how you identify and verify missing data in a loaded DataFrame.


JSON parsing (Chapter 6)
JSON stores data in nested structures — objects (dictionaries) and arrays (lists) that can contain other objects and arrays. This is more flexible than CSV’s flat tabular format, but it means pd.read_json() does not always produce a clean DataFrame. When JSON data contains nested fields, the approach is to parse the file with json.loads() (or json.load() with a file object), navigate the Python dictionaries and lists to understand the structure, extract the fields you need into a flat list of dictionaries, and pass that list to pd.DataFrame(). This is the same list-of-dictionaries-to-DataFrame conversion you used in Week 6, but the source is a JSON file rather than a manually parsed CSV. Deciding how to represent nested fields — extracting specific values from a dictionary, joining a list into a string — is a design decision that depends on what analysis you need to perform.


DataFrame operations (Chapter 5)
Once your data is in a DataFrame, the analysis uses the same operations you applied in Week 6: .describe() for summary statistics, .sort_values() for rankings, boolean indexing with & for multi-condition filtering, .value_counts() for category counts, and new columns created from calculations on existing columns. The difference is how you got there. In Week 6, you spent significant effort parsing the file before you could begin analysis. In Week 8, pd.read_csv() handles the loading, and you spend more time on the analysis itself.

title: Week 10 Guide
text:
Week 7 introduced pd.read_csv() and the broader family of pd.read_*() functions that replace the manual parsing pipeline with a single function call: open the file, detect the header, infer column types, and return a structured DataFrame. But a loaded DataFrame and an analysis-ready DataFrame are not the same thing. pd.read_csv() faithfully represents what is in the file, including the problems. Real data almost always has them.
Chapter 7 covers the work that happens between loading and analyzing. Data from real sources arrives with missing values where records were never filled in, columns that loaded as text because one placeholder string blocked type inference, duplicate rows from logging errors or data pipeline issues, and string columns where the same value appears with different capitalization or extra whitespace. These are not edge cases. They are normal characteristics of data collected from systems that were not designed with analysis in mind. Cleaning that data before you calculate anything is what makes the results trustworthy.
The Week 10 demo works through a fleet registry dataset that has all of these problems. The demo is organized into three parts: handling missing data, data transformation, and string cleaning. Chapter 7 in the textbook goes further than the demo demonstrates, covering forward and back fill strategies for fillna(), value substitution with replace(), regular expressions for pattern-based string manipulation, and categorical data. The full chapter is worth reading alongside the demo.
Week 10 Demo:

title: Week 10 Guide
text:
Chapter 7 concepts used this week

The gap between loaded and ready
When pd.read_csv() loads a file, it reads what is there without evaluating whether the data is correct. A column where two rows say UNKNOWN instead of a number loads as a text column, because that is what the file contains. A column where one row is blank loads with a NaN in that position. Duplicate rows appear twice because they appear twice in the file. The loading function is doing its job correctly; the problems are in the source data.
This means cleaning is always a distinct step in the workflow, separate from loading. Printing the raw file contents before calling pd.read_csv() is a useful habit. Seeing problems in the raw file confirms they came from the source, not from anything pandas did during loading. After loading, .info() and isnull().sum() show what pandas made of the file and where the gaps are.


How pandas represents missing data
pandas uses NaN (Not a Number) as its sentinel value for missing data. NaN is a floating-point concept, which has an important consequence: any integer column that contains even one blank field cannot stay as an integer type, because the integer type has no representation for “missing.” pandas widens the entire column to float64 to accommodate NaN. If you load a file with a crew size column that has one blank row, that column will show float64 in .info() and all values will display as floats, even the ones that were originally whole numbers. This is a design constraint, not an error.
isnull() returns a boolean result for every cell in the DataFrame, True where a value is missing and False where it is not. Calling .sum() on that result counts the missing values per column. This is useful, but it only detects genuine missing data. A column that contains the string UNKNOWN where a number should be will show zero missing values from isnull(), because UNKNOWN is a valid string. pandas has no way to know it represents the absence of a value. Identifying and handling sentinel strings requires a different approach, covered in the type conversion section below.


Removing versus filling missing data
Once you know where missing values are, you have two choices: remove the rows that contain them, or fill the gaps with a reasonable value.
dropna() removes rows containing missing values. Its default behavior is aggressive: any row with at least one missing value in any column is dropped, even if the missing field is not important to your analysis. The subset parameter limits which columns can trigger a row being dropped. dropna(subset=['crew_size']) only removes rows where crew_size is missing, preserving rows that are missing only less critical fields like launch_year.
fillna() replaces missing values with a value you provide. Filling with a constant is simple but often misleading. A launch year of 0 would be a worse representation of the data than a blank. For numeric measurements, filling with the column mean or median is usually more appropriate. The mean is sensitive to outliers: if one value is far outside the typical range, it pulls the mean away from what a representative fill should be. The median is resistant to outliers, because it is the middle value when sorted and does not shift based on extreme values at the ends. When a column has outliers, the median is the safer fill choice.


Type conversion with pd.to_numeric()
When a numeric column contains even one non-numeric string, such as a placeholder like UNKNOWN, a corrupted record, or a formatting artifact, pandas treats the entire column as text during loading. The column appears as object or str in .info(), and you cannot perform calculations on it until it is converted.
pd.to_numeric() converts a Series to a numeric type. The errors parameter controls what happens when a value cannot be converted. The default behavior (errors='raise') throws an exception when it encounters a non-numeric value, which would stop execution. errors='coerce' handles the problem differently: any value that cannot be converted becomes NaN instead of causing an error. After the conversion, the column is a numeric type and the unconvertible strings are now missing values that can be filled using fillna().
This connects to Week 7’s na_values parameter in pd.read_csv(). Both tools solve the same underlying problem: values in the file that cannot be used as-is, but at different stages. na_values addresses the problem during loading by telling pandas which strings to treat as missing. pd.to_numeric(errors='coerce') addresses it after loading, when the column has already arrived as text.


Removing duplicates, recoding values, and renaming columns
These three operations are housekeeping: they make the DataFrame accurate and easier to work with before analysis begins.
drop_duplicates() removes rows that are exact copies of other rows, keeping the first occurrence by default. The subset parameter limits which columns define a duplicate. For example, drop_duplicates(subset=['ship_name']) removes any row where the ship name has already appeared, even if other columns differ. The default behavior compares all columns, which is the right choice when a true duplicate means every field is identical.
map() replaces existing values in a column using a dictionary lookup. It is useful when a column stores compact codes that are not readable in analysis output. Replacing ACT, RET, and MNT with Active, Retired, and Maintenance makes the data self-documenting. Any value in the column that is not a key in the dictionary becomes NaN, so the dictionary needs to cover every value present. This is different from fillna(), which fills blank cells. map() replaces non-null values that are already there.
rename() changes column names using a dictionary where each key is the current name and each value is the new name. You only need to include the columns you want to rename; the rest are left unchanged.


Detecting and replacing outliers
An outlier is a value that is technically present but so far outside the typical range that it distorts any calculation that uses it. The mean is the most common casualty: one extreme value can pull it far enough from the typical range that any calculation depending on it produces misleading results. This matters especially when the plan is to fill missing values with the column mean, because an uncorrected outlier makes the fill just as wrong as the original problem.
describe() is the diagnostic tool. It returns count, mean, standard deviation, min, max, and quartiles for a numeric column. When the max is 9999 and the 75th percentile is 120, the outlier is immediately visible. Boolean indexing then locates the specific row, and .loc assignment replaces the value in place: df.loc[condition, 'column'] = value selects the matching rows and replaces only the specified column. Replacing the outlier with NaN first, then filling with the median, is cleaner than replacing it with the fill value directly. It keeps the two decisions separate: what is wrong, and what to replace it with.


Binning continuous values with pd.cut()
Some analyses are easier with categories than with raw numbers. Rather than working with individual crew sizes, it may be more useful to classify ships as Small, Medium, or Large. pd.cut() creates those categories from a continuous numeric column.
pd.cut() takes a column and a list of bin edges, and assigns each value to the interval it falls in. The labels parameter names the intervals. Because labels name the spaces between edges rather than the edges themselves, there is always one fewer label than there are edges: three edges create two intervals, four edges create three intervals. Values that fall outside all defined bin edges receive NaN rather than being placed in the nearest bin. This is a signal that your bin edges do not cover the full range of your data.
This is why outliers must be addressed before binning. A value of 9999 with bin edges of 0, 30, 100, and 300 would receive NaN from pd.cut() because 9999 exceeds the largest edge. Removing or replacing the outlier first ensures that every valid value lands in a meaningful category.


String cleaning with the str accessor
String columns in real data are rarely consistent. The same ship class might appear as cargo, Cargo, CARGO, or cargo depending on who entered the record and whether they followed any formatting convention. To pandas, those are four different values. Any grouping, filtering, or counting operation on that column will treat them as separate categories and produce wrong results.
pandas provides a str accessor that applies string methods to every value in a Series column at once, without a loop. Methods you have used on individual strings throughout the course, including strip(), lower(), upper(), and title(), are all available through the str accessor, applied column-wide.
The order of operations matters here. str.strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace from each value. It should run first, because a value like Cargo with surrounding spaces is a different string from Cargo, and case standardization alone would not make them match. After stripping, str.lower() converts every value to lowercase, making all variations of the same class name identical. str.contains() then returns a boolean Series showing which rows include a given substring, which works correctly with boolean indexing to filter the DataFrame. If you filter before standardizing case, a search for 'cargo' will miss 'Cargo' and 'CARGO'. Cleaning in the wrong order does not produce an error. It produces results that are silently incomplete.

What is the difference between a DataFrame that has been loaded and one that is ready to analyze?
Why does a numeric column become float64 when it contains one blank field?
Why does isnull() report zero missing values for a column that contains UNKNOWN?
What is the difference between dropna() with no arguments and dropna(subset=[...])?
When is the median a better fill value than the mean, and why?
What does pd.to_numeric(errors='coerce') do when it encounters a value that cannot be converted to a number?
What is the difference between map() and fillna()?
What does pd.cut() assign to a value that falls outside all defined bin edges?
Why must case standardization happen before filtering a string column with str.contains()?

title: Week 11 Guide
text:
Week 11 is a cumulative application of everything from Chapters 1–7. The focus this week is data cleaning: the work that happens between loading a file and being able to trust what it contains. You will apply the Chapter 7 toolkit alongside the loading skills from Week 7 and the DataFrame analysis operations from Weeks 5 and 6. In prior application weeks, data arrived with structural problems: files that needed manual parsing, JSON that needed to be navigated, CSVs with placeholder values that required loading parameters. This week the loading is handled by a single pd.read_csv() call. The work is in what comes after: assessing the data, identifying what is wrong, and resolving each problem before analysis begins.
Assignment:

title: Week 11 Guide
text:
Concepts applied this week

Data cleaning and preparation (Chapter 7)
Real data collected from real systems rarely arrives ready to analyze. Fields get left blank, staff members enter placeholder values instead of leaving cells empty, the same category name gets typed a dozen different ways, records get submitted twice. These are not unusual problems. They are normal characteristics of data that was collected by people under real conditions.
Chapter 7 introduced the tools pandas provides to address each of these problems. This week you apply all of them to a single dataset. You will use isnull().sum() and .info() to assess what the file contains before you touch it. You will use pd.to_numeric() to fix columns that loaded as the wrong type because of placeholder entries. You will use fillna() and dropna() to handle missing values, describe() and .loc to find and replace outliers, drop_duplicates() to remove duplicate records, map() to replace stored codes with readable labels, and the str accessor to standardize inconsistent string columns.
The reason you do this work before analyzing is straightforward: every calculation you run depends on the data being correct. An enrollment count that includes duplicates is wrong. A fee total computed from a column that still has unconverted placeholder values is wrong. Cleaning is not a preliminary step you complete to get to the real work. It is part of the work.


Data loading and analysis (Chapters 5–7)
Loading the file with pd.read_csv() is the starting point. Once the data is clean, the analysis uses the same DataFrame operations you have applied in Weeks 5, 6, and 8: boolean indexing, .value_counts(), .sort_values(), and pd.cut() for grouping continuous values into labeled categories. The analytical questions are not where the new challenge lives this week. Getting to them with data you can trust is.
